author: hty253
title: switch to anyone else if your instructor is HU Jisan
comment_teaching:
Be prepared to 100% self-learning if you failed to swap to other instructors. I tried hard to attend every class at first and eventually acknowledged the reality that it is a waste of my time. Trust me, go sleep in the library will be a better option if you have no idea how to spend your time.

Apart from his accent and the fact that he often speaks Mandarin hence leaves all international students confused, the biggest problem is that he teaches in such an unclear, illogic, and un-systematized way. I learned ZERO from him.

The other thing that really irritated me is his attitude towards his teaching. When all other professors strived to provide high-quality online courses and spared no effort to keep us following up with the latest arrangement of the school under this unfortunate circumstance. Mr. HU Jishan did NOT offer a single piece of information, no learning materials, no recording videos, even no message like "Hey we're not going to have class tmr!" FOR 17 DAYS, that's nearly two and a half weeks ^_^ And incredibly, right after I complained about this via the school online survey on the very 16th day, he provided us with recording (= the lowest quality video I have ever seen) on the next day!!! Whoa, what a big surprise huh?

------
(for the self-learning part)
Find out the topics you are required to learn on the homework page and go learn them from khan academy. It is totally enough for 1013. I had zero knowledge in calculus before and got 84 (mean 58.4) in mid-term with the help of khan academy only.
comment_grading: Fair grading, all based on the absolute score (mid-term, final, and assignments.)
comment_workload: There are 8 assignments this semester, which you can try as many times as you wish before the deadline. The overall workload is acceptable.

author: whalewhalewhale
comment_content:
Content is similar to HKDSE if you learn M2, but I think M1 students can manage, since they already have the main skills and concepts for differentiation and integration, which is the biggest coverage of the course.

- Differentiation
- Integration
- Mean Value Theorem
- Intermediate Value Theorem
- Absolute Function
- Limit
- L'Hopital Rule

- Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
comment_teaching: Not very good, to be honest. comment_grading:

author: william_chk
comment_content:
Mostly M2 stuff together with a few theorems related to derivatives, e.g. L'Hopital's Rule. mean value theorem, etc

Week 1 Review of the concepts of functions and graphs.  (1.2-1.3; Appendix A.)
Week 2 Elementary functions.  (1.5-1.6; Appendix D.)
Week 3 Tangent and velocity; the limit of  a function and limit laws.  (2.1-2.3.)
Week 4 Continuity; limit at infinity; limit definition of derivative and rates of change.  (2.5-2.7.)
Week 5 Derivatives of basic functions; differentiation rules.  (2.8, 3.1-3.3.)
Week 6 Chain rule and implicit differentiation.  (2.8, 3.1-3.3.)
Week 7 Rates and related rates.  (3.7-3.9.)
Week 8 Linear approximation and differentials; Newton's method; Maximum and minimum values.  (3.10, 4.8, 4.1.)
Week 9 Mean Value Theorem; derivatives and the shape of a graph; L'Hopital's Rule.  (4.2-4.4.)
Week 10 Curve sketching; optimization problems.  (4.5-4.7.)
Week 11 Antiderivatives; areas and distance travelled.  (4.9, 5.1.)
Week 12 Definite integrals; Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.  (5.2-5.3.)
Week 13 Net change; substitution rule.  (5.4-5.5.)
comment_teaching:
Mainly writes on whiteboard in class, in most situation jotting down notes is needed. Teaching is also supplemented with learning outcomes & examples that will be posted on course website periodically.

Professor Lam clearly prepared thoroughly for class and could teach without drawing any reference to his notes throughout the whole lecture.
comment_grading:
quite good
The grading reflects how much you work on this course
comment_workload:
Not too high, webwork is the only required thing, around 10 questions, once per week.
Lectures and tutorials are optional. No attendance taken.
And for the webwork, you may try as many times as you like, as long as you get the correct answer at last, full credit is given.

author: Wong Kui Kwan
comment_content:
This is an introductory course in one-variable calculus, the first in the Calculus I and II
sequence, designed for students who passed HKDSE Mathematics Extended Part M1 or M2.
Major topics include: functions and their limits, continuity, derivatives and rules of
differentiation, applications of derivatives, basic integral calculus.

Week 1 Basic set theory, numbers and intervals, Inequalities and absolute values
Week 2 Functions and graphs, Elementary functions
Week 3 Limits of a function, Rules of limits
Week 4 Infinite limits and limit at infinity, Continuity
Week 5 Derivatives, Rules of differentiation, chain rule
Week 6 Techniques of differentiation
Week 7 Rates of change, Linear approximations, differentials
Week 8 Newton’s method, Maximum and minimum of functions
Week 9 Mean value theorem, ľHôpital’s rule
Week 10 Curve sketching Optimization
Week 11 Areas and integrals, Newton-Leibniz formula, antiderivatives
Week 12 Substitution rule, Integration by parts
Week 13 Final review

Reference texts:
J. Stewart, D. Clegg and S. Watson, Calculus: Early Transcendentals (Metric version, 9th
edition), Cengage.
J. Hu, W. Li and Y. Wu, Calculus for scientists and engineers with Matlab
comment_teaching: No comment, mostly self-study
comment_grading:
WeBWorK assignments (10%):
9 in total in this semester, weekly in the last month, 2 weeks
Extra problem sets (0%)
Midterm Test (35%):
Final Exam (55%)
cover everything that has been taught in the course.

Grading Range according to outline:
The assignment of letter grades is criterion-referenced
exact “grade boundaries” vary due to the difficulty of the assessments,
85% or above for A-/A/A+,
70% or above for B-/B/B+
40% or above for a passing grade.

My score:
around 67-68%, got B-
Midterm: slightly above the mean
Final: a few points above mean
Assignments: Nearly full score

reasonable in my perspective, but difficult to get A range
comment_workload:
It should not be too tough if you put enough time into studying this course (which I did not)

If you can handle and understand the assignments on your own, you have already built a strong foundation. Ideally, you have to finish the provided tutorial questions and the extra problem set to keep up with the difficulty and the problem-solving speed expected in the exams.

The main workload is just practice and practice. Do maths.

author: Draymond
comment_content: MATH1013 Calculus I is a 3-credit introductory course in one-variable calculus for students with a strong pre-calculus background. The curriculum covers fundamental concepts including functions, limits, continuity, derivatives with differentiation rules, applications of derivatives (such as optimization and curve sketching), and an introduction to integral calculus. Student performance is assessed through regular WeBWorK assignments (10%), a midterm test (35%), and a cumulative final exam (55%), with a strong emphasis on conceptual understanding and computational proficiency.
comment_teaching: The professor demonstrates strong technical expertise in calculus but struggles to convey concepts in an accessible way. Lectures often involve reading directly from prepared notes with minimal interaction, leaving students to piece together complex ideas without clear, step-by-step explanations.
comment_grading: Already mentioned, and not absolute grading.
comment_workload: Substantial and designed to require consistent effort throughout the semester. Students are expected to keep pace with a structured weekly schedule covering topics from basic set theory to integration by parts over 13 weeks. The assessment scheme includes regular WeBWorK assignments (worth 10%) that must be completed online, alongside optional but strongly encouraged extra problem sets intended to provide sufficient practice beyond the often easier online exercises.

comment_grading:
no attendance needed
grade consists of 3 parts: WeBWork homework 10%, midterm 35%, final 55%. good students get good grades, that's it
comment_workload: light workload. To get 10% marks of this course, you only need to finish around 16 problems per week on WeBWork.I reckon it is acceptable.

You need to get above 85 percent total to get A range. But you can check your paper after exams and the checking is pretty lenient in my opinion.

author: Dcch
title: yo
comment_content:
The content of the course is fairly standard, similar to most calculus classes. It covers essential topics, but nothing particularly exceptional stood out. Overall, it meets the basic expectations.
Week 1 Review of numbers, inequalities and absolute values, functionsAppendix A;  Sections 1.1-1.3
Week 2 Exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithms
Sections 1.4-1.5
Week 3 Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws
Sections 2.1-2.4
Week 4 Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes
Sections 2.5-2.6
Week 5 Derivatives and rates of change, basic derivatives, product and quotient rules
Sections 2.7-2.8, 3.1-3.2
Week 6 Derivatives of (inverse) trigonometric and logarithmic functions, chain rule, implicit differentiation
Sections 3.2-3.6
Week 7 Rates of change problems, related rates
Sections 3.8-3.9
Week 8 Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values
Sections 3.10-3.11, 4.1
Week 9 Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and the shape of a graph, L‘Hopital’s rule
Sections 4.2-4.4
Week 10 Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s method
Sections 4.5-4.8
Week 11 Anti-derivatives, areas and distances, definite integrals
Sections 4.9, 5.1-5.2
Week 12 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, average value of a function
Section 5.3
Week 13 Applications of integrals, substitution rule
Sections 5.4-5.5
comment_teaching: Mainlander professor with a heavy Chinese accent. Never attended any lectures except the first and the final review session. They gave exercise and step-by-step solutions, which can be found in canvas. Just go and download them from Canvas if you want to practice.
comment_grading:
I am not sure about the grading but i think is normal

WeBWorK Online Homework: 10%
Midterm Exam: 35%
Final Exam: 55%

(there are no attendance for the tutorial.)
comment_workload: Homework is not that difficult if you study m2. The deadline of the hw is not 2359 so please remember to check when you need to hand in those hw.

author: hjy
title: Easy course
comment_content: 内容不难，前半个学期是复习高中知识，期中考试之后基本上就是高中内容的拓展，涉及积分，极限。有一些专有名词和定理的名字要特别记忆，和1023有区别。
comment_teaching: 这个老师讲的不好，基本上就是读笔记（没见有PPT），而且似乎教学经验不充足，手写笔记存在错误，讲课节奏较平。但是老师平易近人，有问题回邮件比较快。此外，T堂讲的没有太大用，就是纯演算，可以不去，不考勤。
comment_grading: 挺公正的，正常学，不下很大功夫也能拿A。A+要比较细心，对概念理解要比较深刻。拿B可能没好好学。完全不听课纯靠考前突击，考试大概80～85/100.
comment_workload: 作业比课堂讲义难，比problem set简单（这个不计分，做拓展训练用）。作业也不是很多，回答次数无限制，但是一次写超过一份作业会比较累。部分不会写的题目可以用AI。越往后作业越难。

author: Ultima_Franklin
comment_content:
Basically M2 stuff.
Limits (continuity and differentiability)
Differentiation rules
Theories
Optimizations problem
Indefinite and definite Integration (with basic substitution skills and integration by parts)

The course also includes new theories (e g. mean value theorem) extra technique upon evaluating limit such as L'Hopital's rule.

I recommend going to the lecture. Some of the exams question are come from the lecture. If you are strong enough, it is fine to skip it.
comment_teaching:
Instructor is nice, however he's voice is low and he may make some mistakes during lecture. If you go to his lecture I would recommend sitting close to him. Also, talk to him after the lecture, he would help you if you have any problem.
To be honest, his lecture is kinda boring, so it is ok to skip the lecture. However you may miss some examples that appear similarly in exam.
The tutorial basically focuses on solving problems. You cannot learn too much from tutorial but you can train your problem-solving skills.
comment_grading:
Exams are not too difficult for this year. In my opinion it is challenging but not that hard. So I got an A+. However I would say it is hard for student to get an A grade if they didn't study M2.

You cannot use calculator, so you better train your arithmetic skills.
comment_workload:
You only have to do online assignment.
Not hard but it is complicated, some of the questions are misleading. However, try not to use AI to do, because doing assignment can strengthen your foundementals.

No attendance for anything, you can skip everything theoretically.

author: KelseyYSQ
comment_content: L1 was the math session that focuses more on mathematical proof rather than just calculation. I struggle occasionally to understand, but the course really helps me with building a firm system that I didn't have before.
comment_teaching: Excellent teaching, clear and fluent. The course doesn't require attendance but I am happy to listen to Henry's lectures.
comment_grading: Grading isn't too harsh, the TAs try to give you as much points as possible. It's math so I'd say it is objective.

author: pranshu

comment_grading:

author: Harvey_D
comment_content:
In my humble opinion, it's a very basic calculus course. Three-fifths of the knowledge is actually covered in DSE's M2 curriculum. Therefore, it should be relatively easy for those with an M2 background.
Chapter 1 :  Function
Chapter 2 :  Limit
Chapter 3 : Derivatives
Chapter 4 : Applications of derivatives
Chapter 5 : Integrals

author: JasonTT
title: Not a easy course for students who just study M1
comment_content: This course mainly focuses on the differentiation and integration at the M2 level, and covers all content required for M2. Therefore, if you have only studied the  M1 syllabus, you will need to allocate extra time to review, think independently, and practice extensively. Most of your peers are already familiar with this material and find it rather straightforward, but it will present a considerable challenge for you.
comment_teaching: I took the course taught by Professor Harry. He responds to questions very promptly and thoroughly, and replies to emails at a fast speed. He is also very welcoming and encouraging when students approach him with doubts. Every part of his lectures is clearly explained and well-structured.
comment_grading: Regarding grading and academic performance, since I had only studied M1 and did not complete enough practice exercises, my final result was far from satisfactory.
comment_workload: In terms of workload, an online assignment is assigned every one to two weeks. At the beginning of the course, each assignment consists of approximately 10 questions, and the overall workload is relatively light in the first few weeks. As this is a mathematics course, you may use various tools to verify your answers and look up solution methods. As the course progresses into later weeks, each assignment will contain around 13 questions and typically take about two hours to finish. Some problems are relatively lengthy, while others demand strong graph-reading and interpretation skills. Therefore, consistent independent practice and focused attention during lectures are essential to master the solution approaches.

comment_workload: Workload is okay, WebWork questions almost every week, then mt, finals.

author: Guanbo Wang
comment_content: The course covers the fundamental concepts of calculus in a clear and structured way. The content is generally appropriate for an introductory-level course and helps build a solid foundation for more advanced mathematics.
comment_teaching: The instructor explains the material clearly and follows a logical progression. Lectures are well-organized, though the pace may feel fast at times for students without a strong prior background.
comment_grading: Grading standards are relatively strict, and it can be challenging to earn high marks even when the material is understood. Partial credit is limited, so small mistakes can significantly affect scores
comment_workload: The overall workload is manageable and not excessive. Homework and assessments are reasonable for a foundational calculus course and align well with the material covered in class.

comment_content:
MATH1013L L01 is a math major based Calculus1.
Unless you're from mainland or your math is actually good, swap to a normal Calculus course.

Content is almost the same, exam difficulty is not.
More focused on proving equations rather than application.

C0: Preliminaries, Logic, Basic Set Theory, Inequalities, Mathematical Induction, Sigma Notation
C1: Functions
C2: Limits
C3: Derivatives
C4: Mean Value Theorem
comment_teaching:
Great Lecturer, Explained Everything Clearly and In Detail.

Notes given are useful, very detailed.
comment_grading:
Written Assignments - 16%

Finals - 42%

The A Grade for L01 is just a few marks lesser than normal Calculus I lectures, but the difficulty is not the same.

Decent, 1 Assignment every 10 Days.
Could be finished on time, not very heavy

author: Lucccccyyyyy
title: harddddddddd
comment_content: teaching some theoretical knowledge of mathematics
comment_teaching: The teacher's lecture in class was very clear. More complex theoretical knowledge points are usually explained through corresponding exercises.
comment_grading: The grades will be calculated based on the results of the class assignments, mid-term exams and final exams respectively. The content of the exam is still quite challenging, but we will use last year's questions as a reference.
comment_workload: There will be class assignments every two weeks. Each assignment usually consists of about 7 or 8 questions and it will take a considerable amount of time to complete them.

author: 9SKY
comment_content: The grading system of this course is fair, transparent and reasonable, with clear evaluation criteria for every assignment and exam stated at the beginning. Grades are based on actual learning performance, and the professor values both process and results equally, giving constructive feedback on all work to help us improve. There’s no unfair scoring, and hard work and active participation truly pay off. This fair grading not only motivates us to study earnestly but also makes us focus more on learning itself rather than just chasing scores, which is really meaningful for our long-term growth.
comment_teaching: This course has been an absolute delight, thanks to Professor Henry’s exceptional teaching. His/Her lectures are well-organized, insightful and highly engaging, with clear, thorough explanations that make even complex theories easy to grasp. The professor is also patient in answering questions, always inspiring us to think deeply and explore the subject further. I’ve gained tremendous knowledge and practical insights from this course, and it has truly sparked my greater interest in the field. An outstanding learning experience overall!
comment_grading: many great students in this course ,so it's not an easy deal to get A+
comment_workload: The course workload is well-balanced—neither too heavy to cause pressure nor too light to lack challenge. Assignments are carefully designed to match the lecture content, focusing on consolidating key knowledge instead of tedious busy work. The amount of reading, homework and projects is manageable with proper time planning, and each task has clear learning objectives. This balanced workload lets us absorb knowledge thoroughly, finish work with high quality and even have time for in-depth exploration of the subject, making the learning process efficient and enjoyable without burnout.

author: zhoujinwen

comment_content: Very beginner-friendly, and it helps you understand the fundamental knowledge of calculus and functions
comment_teaching: The role of the instructor in this course is not particularly prominent; they mainly serve as a guide. Since I haven't attended all the instructors' classes, I won't make any judgments about them.
comment_grading: If you have studied M2, you could likely achieve a grade of around B in this course with minimal effort, and reaching B+ or A would be possible with a bit of focused study..For the midterm and final exams in my year, the difficulty was fairly consistent, and the question types were relatively predictable. I'd recommend practicing more with past exam papers.
comment_workload: The workload isn't heavy, and the assignments are relatively simple, but you might need to spend some time after class understanding certain slightly more challenging types of problems on your own.

comment_teaching: He is a nice professor. But I just don’t know what he is doing in lectures. He does math on paper with no interaction with students. His lecture notes are very messy and difficult to read. His voice and writings are also very unclear. I can’t learn any concepts from lectures.
comment_grading:

F < 40% < D < 50% < C’s < 70% < B’s < 85% < A’s
Note that these are NOT "guaranteed letter grades"; they are subject to slight changes. And our course is NOT graded on a curve, but is graded by criterion referencing. author: FFer
comment_content: The course covers the standard first-semester calculus topics—limits, differentiation, applications, and basic integration. The material is rigorous and theoretically solid, which is good preparation for later math courses, but it moves quickly and assumes strong algebra background. If you're comfortable with high-school math, it's manageable; otherwise, it can feel intense.
comment_teaching: The professor explains concepts clearly, works through plenty of examples in lecture, and posts detailed notes online. Attendance isn't mandatory but helpful because the examples are often better than the textbook. Office hours are useful—he answers questions patiently. Not the most engaging style, but effective and professional.
comment_grading:

author: hwyh
comment_content: The content of L01 is rigorous and theory‑driven, clearly designed for students intending to major in mathematics. Instead of focusing mainly on computational techniques, the course emphasizes logical reasoning, foundational concepts, and proof writing. Topics such as logic, set theory, injectivity/surjectivity, limits, continuity, differentiability, and the mean value theorem are treated with a level of depth that goes beyond standard introductory calculus. This approach builds strong mathematical maturity and prepares students well for higher‑level pure math courses. However, students expecting a more application‑oriented or formula‑driven calculus course may find the abstract style challenging.
comment_teaching: Dr. Cheng adopts a clear, structured, and concept‑focused teaching style. His lectures prioritize understanding over memorization, often explaining the motivation behind definitions and the logic behind proofs. The course environment encourages students to think critically and engage with the material at a deeper level. Office hours are generous and flexible, and the instructor seems approachable and supportive. The TAs also play an important role, especially in tutorials where students practice writing proofs and clarifying conceptual difficulties.
comment_grading: The grading scheme is heavily weighted toward the midterm and final exam (42% each), with written assignments making up the remaining 16%. The assignments emphasize clarity of reasoning and proof writing rather than routine calculations, which aligns with the course philosophy. The lowest homework score is dropped, which helps reduce pressure. Because the exams are long, closed‑book, and conceptually demanding, students need a solid grasp of the material rather than surface‑level familiarity. Overall, the grading is fair but challenging, rewarding students who genuinely understand the concepts.
comment_workload: The workload is moderate to heavy depending on your background. Students with strong mathematical foundations (e.g., HKDSE M1/M2 or prior exposure to proofs) will find the pace manageable, though still intellectually demanding. Those new to abstract reasoning may need to invest significant time outside class to digest the material. Homework assignments require careful writing and cannot be rushed. While there is no WeBWork component for L01, the depth of the written assignments and the conceptual nature of the exams mean consistent weekly effort is essential.

comment_teaching: His teaching was clear, but the pace was too fast to non-DSE-M2 students like me.
comment_grading:
As I just mentioned, I only pulled some all-nighters before the midterm and final, and I probably started studying two weeks before the midterm, so my grade wouldn't seem good to y'all.

webwork (10%): 10/10
midterm (35%): 59/100

overall: around 62% --> C+

I thought my grade would be poorer, like a C or C-, or even a fail before my scores were released. But to my surprise the C+ range was broad, because if you want to get a B-, you'll probably need a 70% overall.
comment_workload:
1 webwork per week
You could ask AI to help you, but sometimes they weren't accurate, so you had to calculate by yourself.

Each webwork took me at least 2-3 hours to finish. Sometimes when it came to difficult topics, I used much more time.

A few questions had bugs (e.g., there's smth. wrong with the graph), so you had to spot it and repeat trial and error to acquire the correct answer.

___________________________________________

However, when a DSE M1 student who got level 5 only studies a course that contains 90% M2, they must put a lot of effort into the course.

I had to skip live lectures and watch the videos at home instead. I spent at least 3 hours watching each video, except for the parts I have learnt before. I didn’t realise I had other options until I tried watching YouTube videos—suddenly, I could understand a concept in just 30 minutes, instead of spending three hours listening and still feeling confused. But that was 4 days before the final, so if you are lucky enough to read my post, make sure you try watching YouTube videos if you think you will get overwhelmed by this course. Or else, you wouldn't acquire the grade that's proportional to your effort.

Frankly, I didn't know how much effort I had to put in to get a good grade, and I was really lost in this course. 3 hours and 3 hours of videos, but I wasn't productive. So, I had to admit that I kind of gave up midway. I only pulled some all-nighters before the midterm and final.

author: livemyownlife
comment_content:
This course covers basic single-variable calculus: limits and continuity, derivatives and differentiation rules, applications of derivatives (like max/min problems, related rates, optimization, Newton's method), and a basic intro to integration, like substitution rule and integration by parts. Around 60-70% of the material overlaps with DSE M2, but the exam questions go deeper than hw rather than just calculations. Some new topics include the Squeeze Theorem, L'Hôpital's Rule, Mean Value Theorem, Linear Approximation, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

You really need to understand the concepts clearly, not just memorize steps. If your understanding is only surface-level, the exam can easily screw you up because they change the question style and test whether you actually get the theory. Don't just rely on homework. The exam is way harder.
comment_teaching:
The overall teaching quality varies a lot depending on which professor you get. For the non-L1 sections, many students report heavy mainland accents that make lectures hard to follow. Some professors I heard just read slides without much explanation, which isn't super helpful. Tutorial sessions can be more useful than lectures, especially if you get a good TA.
​
Don't rely too much on going to lectures, especially if your professor has a strong Chinglish accent that's hard to understand. It's better to self-study following the course schedule or watch Henry Cheng's lecture videos. Many students from other sections sit in on his classes or watch his recordings because he explains things way more clearly. His notes and problem sets really help your exam.
comment_grading:
This course uses absolute grading, which means your grade depends on your own score, not on how others perform. The usual breakdown is roughly: 10% homework (WeBWorK), 35% midterm, and 55% final. The typical cutoffs are around 85%+ for A range, 70-85% for B range. How much effort you put in is how much grade you get, pretty straightforward.
​
2025 Fall Mid-term:
Mean = 65.37, SD = 20.63
Highest = 100, UQ = 82, Median = 67.5, LQ = 51

2025 Fall Final:
Mean = 62.96, SD = 19.85
Highest = 100, UQ = 78, Median = 65.5, LQ = 49
comment_workload:
The workload is not too heavy compared to other year-1 courses. You mainly just have weekly WeBWorK homework, which is pretty manageable. Most questions are standard, and there are unlimited attempts for each question except MCQs, so you can keep trying until you get full marks. There are also optional extra problem sets like the ones from Henry, that are more exam-level. If you want a good grade, you should do them, but overall this is not a high workload course as long as you keep up week by week.
​

Homework is easy and doesn't take much time. Just don't forget the deadlines since they're not always on Canvas. The real time investment is if you want to do well on exams, then you need to practice past papers and problem sets.

author: Deyan
title: L1 review
comment_content: Same as the other sections. This is important in exams and assignments, as points are deducted for wrong logic, even if the answer and the method are generally correct.

author: 白给
title: I LOVE MATH 1013!!! SUNSHINE AND TOMORROW!!!
comment_content:
Chapter 0 Preliminaries
Chapter 1 Functions
Chapter 2 Limits and continuity
Chapter 3 Derivatives
Chapter 4 Applications of derivatives
Chapter 5 Integrals: an informal introduction

Half of the content is just M2 stuff. Another half of the content is new.
Comparing to M2 curriculum, the content is more rigorous, you need to learn more theorem and principles (e.g. Mean Value Theorem, Intermediate Value theorem, etc.), and you need to know how to use them.

For me, I don't think the content is very tough, it is a bit challenging but still okay.
I love Math, so I give an A, you might disagree with me, but I don't care XD
comment_teaching:
Instructor: Liu Junbang

Not good.
He prepared his note before the class, then simply copy his note again by a pen with useless and boring elaboration in his lecture. There is no interaction in the class.
If you studied M2 before and plan to self-study all the content, he can be a good choice, no attendance is recorded.
But if you indeed want to learn something from the lecture, my idea is get rid of him, and read Henry's note (all MATH 1013 students can access the content page of all instructors of MATH 1013).

Anyway, he is a kind instructor. You won't feel pressure if you ask him question after the class.
When I am doing final paper checking, he approves my request on redoing a MC question on the whiteboard, and explain the concept kindly when I struggled.

To conclude, not good teaching, but a kind instructor, so I give a C

author: mymbtiisrest
title: Easy to pass, hard to master, but light workload
comment_content:
Basically DSE M2 with a little bit more explanation behind some concepts/theories.
Those with DSE M2 Level 5 or above should be able to handle most of the topics easily

Chapter 0 - high school stuff
Chapter 1 - Functions (composites, inverses)
Chapter 2 - Limits (continuity)
Chapter 3 - Derivatives
Chapter 4 - Application of Derivatives (LHopitals, MVT, IVT, Rolle’s theorem)
Chapter 5 - Introduction to Integrals

I will say basically Chapter 1&2 are new from high school, but easy to handle; while 70% of ch3-5 is learnt in DSE M2.
Those with DSE M1 might struggle a little bit, but honestly you’ll do just fine by doing the exercises and problem sets. A range would be more strict but B range should be achievable for all.
comment_teaching:
Tbh whoever teaches you doesn’t matter since you get access to videos/slides/notes from other courses in Canvas - but I highly recommend Henry.
Henry is a fun and approachable professor, very helpful in answering your questions, and fluent in Cantonese English and Mandarin. His classes are quite interesting (though I skipped most of them LOL). He just has good vibes and is a good professor as well!
comment_grading:
10% Webwork Assignments (unlimited attempts, each one takes around 1-2 hours, just complete them and you will get a free 10%)
35% Midterm
55% Finals

My grade:
Webwork Full
Midterm 86/100
Final 87.5/100
Overall: ~88%
Final Grade: A
Honestly much more lenient than expected since I expected an A-. For grading aim for 85% for A range
And midterms are relatively easy - try to aim higher in midterms because the finals are going to be hard.  finals cover mostly ch3 onwards but is still cumulative.  Just be careful with some careless mistakes - it might cost a lot of your marks
comment_workload:
Light workload.
Webwork assignments are given two weeks before the deadline, so unless you cramp everything you have time to do it
The only thing I recommend is finish all the past papers and extra problem sets before your final. Try to clear them throughout the course so you don’t need to cramp it. Tutorial notes are somewhat helpful, but some of them might be not appropriate (too easy/too hard) so just do the ones that you think are suitable.
But overall there’s really nothing heavy, just study a little bit harder before exams and you’re going to do just fine. Shd be easy for ppl with M2 background, but also manageable for ppl without them as well

author: USTSlave
comment_content:
Math1013 is basically Calc 1.

Limits and Continuity (IVT, L'Hospital)

The Derivative (Definition and Basic Rules)

Applications of Derivatives (Rates, Optimization, Graphing) [EVT, MVT, Rolle's Theorem]

Integrals (Definition and Fundamental Theorem)

Applications of Integrals (Areas, Net Change)

Introduction to Differential Equations (Simple separable equations).



Although, they included some new content this year, including integration by parts (which was not included before). And, they removed content such as Riemann Sum.

author: Ariel Ip
title: Non-L1 Review
comment_content:
M2 with additional differentiation techniques (linear approximation and Newton's method), l'Hopital's rule and some theorems such as the mean value theorem.

If you learn M2 beforehand (don't know about MATH1003), the difficulty is acceptable. If not, you'll have to catch up.

As for the detailed curriculum, check the other reviews.
comment_teaching: The teacher and teaching assistants went over the notes with little student interaction, but are willing to answer questions. If you don't want to attend, just read the notes from lectures and tutorials (and also Stewart's Calculus).
comment_grading:
WeBWorK 10%, Midterm 35%, Final 55%. No calculators allowed for exams.

Both exams contain MCs and long questions, with some requiring proofs. The course is graded absolutely, so be careful (especially for MCs) and do past papers beforehand.

No attendance requirement for both lectures and tutorials.
comment_workload:
9 Webworks, with a deadline of 4 weeks each.

Some questions might be tricky to answer (and you can't copy others' work as the exact numbers might be different), but as long as you leave enough time and ask around, the workload is light.

comment_content:
Similar to HKDSE M2 calculus syllabus and the only differ is that it has sightly more theorems to remember (and those theorems are very trivial). It just basically a review of m2 with extra learning (actually recommend skipping all lectures and self learn if u have lv5+ in m2 and referring to grading, I do skip all lectures but ended up such grade).
There may exist people like me, not attending lectures and not even reading the lecture notes, that is feasible but not recommended as the theorems are new, you still have to remember the names (or else you would be like me that the only long question part in the final wrong is about stating the theorems).

For 25-26 Fall Final Paper, I don't know what is the purpose of the paper. It appears more likely to examine your memorisation ability. The paper is shitted that there is a question where you basically have to memorise all shit from the notes and copy and paste. And due to my poor memorising ability, I successfully scored 0 on that diabolical question while having full marks for other long questions. I do not get why the fuck the department made this paper so shit that all you have to do for that 5-6 points is just memorise shit. That just basically contradicts with the purpose of hosting math course.
comment_teaching:
Her lectures are decent but not worthy to attend every lecture. I personally think that she indeed able to teach the knowledges clearly but I would rather self learn.
Also, I don't know for what reason, but we are the latest one to receive our grade and the midterm paper checking arrangement was a little bit "surprising".
comment_grading:
Absolute grading, no matter how high you are above UQ, u have a chance that u would not get A (I am B+ with 83.5 which I was 3 marks higher than UQ in final, and midterm too). Normally, as long as you have a normal intelligent, I think you should be ok with the homework, I mean it can be done with ease as mostly are infinitely many tries.
The grading range would be adjusted depending on the difficulty of the papers, but don't expect it to be lower by a lot. However, it is still safe to get A range if u pass 85 (usually wouldn't raise the line).

Overall, I prefer relative grading over absolute grading as my above UQ does nothing here.
comment_workload:
Refer back to "Grading" session, the homework are relatively easy and the deadline are very long, usually a month or something, if you find the assignments difficult to finish in time, I think you should find problems yourself first I guess? Not to be mean but you will understand once you start.
For tutorial, I don't know what's the point of attending the tutorials and it has no attendance so I skipped all tutorials, unless you really struggle with some knowledges, or else I would not recommend attending tutorials especially those at 9am to 12pm.

author: MVPMarco
comment_content:
Basically M2 Stuff
I've studied DSE M1 before, and I think that extra effort for understanding M2 content is needed to study this course.
I think if you puts enough effort into this course, it will not be a huge obstacle for you, the content is not too difficult for M1 students, but it needs understanding. Very conceptual content.
But if you're bad at mathematics or you didn't study M1/2 before, then good luck to you.
comment_teaching:
Instructor: Quan, Xueyang
I think her teaching is fine; she explains the concept clearly and fluently. She may not be the best instructor I've met, but she's definitely far from the worst one.
A little bit of accent, but it is acceptable and understandable.
Willing to answer your questions in Mandarin and English. Would even reply to you via email to explain some complicated questions and concepts, so ask her if you have any questions.

TA: Felix Kwok
No comments to TA since he is just demostrating home work questions on tutorials and I often finish hw before it. So I seldom attend tutorials.
comment_grading:
The grading is fine and far enough
Homework (Webwork) 10%
Midterm 35%
Final 55%
Absolute grading, related to the effort you put in.

>= 85% A-
>= 70% B-

>= 40% Pass

My result (fyi):
Homework (Webwork) 10% 10/10
Midterm 35% 65.5/100 (Mean: 65.37, S.D.: 20.63; ~= Mean)
Final 55% 77.5/100 (Mean: 62.96, S.D.: 19.85, ~+0.73 S.D.)

Total 75.5/100
B
DSE both Core and M1 Lv5
comment_workload:
9 Webwork (~1 per week)

Unlimited trials for most of the questions, so you should be able to gain the free 10% from homework.

Doing past papers and additional problem sets to prepare for exams would be fine enough for you to survive this course.

But pay more attention on understanding the concepts clearly if you aim A range.

Overall the workload depends on your target, but generally not large.

author: Zenas
title: L1
comment_content: 这门课是微积分1，我想读数学系所以读了L1，难度上来讲是要比非L1难的当然比1023简单不少。考试有60多分的证明（可以说section 2全是证明题）前两章讲的都比较简单，主要是数列相关的和简单的一些求极限，最后两章讲的是MVT罗尔那些乱七八糟的然后让你证明一堆东西MT平均60，final平均50。本人通过AL进入UST 这门课貌似对国际学生不太友好（因为高考的怪物太吓人了）
comment_teaching: Henry讲的还是很不错的，课下有任何不回的问题他都能耐心解答，准备考试的话把PP和lecture notes还有problem set看好足够A range。Henry真的好评，TA上课的文件对自己的知识漏洞检测还是不错的，虽然有时候题比考试要难一点的，但是很锻炼思维。
comment_grading:
绝对龟 85 A+ 80A 75A-
homework16% MT42% final42%（个人感觉MT好拿分）
comment_workload: 一共8次作业 一次50分 基本全是证明题 对于学生的逻辑严谨度是个很大考验 考试也是如此，本人严谨度不算很好在作业和考试扣了一些分，大家一定要注意

author: TUbo
title: Good grade
comment_content:
Most contents are covered by DSE M2, including limits and differentiation, and some very basic integration. The new stuffs include:
Piecewise Function and Absolute Values
Continuity
Intermediate, Extreme, Mean Value Theorem
Linear Approximation
Newton's Method
L'Hopital Rule
Relative Error

For students who studied calculus before they will find it relatively easy to handle.
comment_teaching: I only went to lecture twice, the professor has a strong accent but notes are written quite well. Reading notes from different instructors and doing the extra problem set are way more effective than attending lectures in my opinion.
comment_grading:
10% Webwork
35% Midterm
55% Final

Webwork is a website for homework which only requires the numerial value answer, so you can basically just use AI. No time limit and unlimited attempts which in other words, as long as you are aware of the deadline, you will earn the 10%.

The midterm is very easy for me. I scored a mid-90 on it. 14 MCs (each worth 4 marks) and 3 "long questions". The topics are just very basic differentiations, limits and continuity.

The final is slightly harder as there are way more trickier MC questions. I got wrong for about 4 of them while got all LQ correct, but they already made my grade drop to slightly below 90s.

The mean for both midterm and final are suprisingly low:
MT - Mean = 65.37, SD = 20.63, UQ = 82, Median = 67.5, LQ = 51
F - Mean = 62.96, SD = 19.85, UQ = 78, Median = 65.5, LQ = 49
There are students who scored 100 on both papers btw...

My final grade: A (final mark 90.5, A+ cutoff is 92.5X)
comment_workload: Basically 0 workload, it purely depends on your efficency on self-studying some parts of the 2nd half of the course.

author: wg
title: Not good but not bad
comment_content:
Review of some topics from high school: Sets, Solving inequalities in one variable, sigma
Functions, compositions of two functions, inverse function
Basic functions, exponential, logarithm, trigonometry functions and their inverses
Limits, continuity of a function, rules to compute limits, the natural number e
Squeeze theorem, limits involving trigonometric functions. Continuity of a function
Derivative of a function, tangent to a graph
3 typical kinds of non-differentiable functions: non-continuous, sharp conner, and vertical tangent.  Basic derivatives rules for elementary fucntions( power functions, exp/log, sin/cos.
Derivatives for sums, products, quotients, compositions, and differentiate equations(implicit differentiation), derivatives for inverse functions
Tricks on computing limits
Change of rate, related rates problem
Linear approximations, Newton's method
Mean value theorem, and its applications
L'Hospital Rule
Sketch the graph of a function, slant asymptotes
Optimization problem
Indefinite integral
Area, definite integral
Fundamental theorem of Calculus/Newton-Leibniz formula
Substitution Rule
"The philosophy of Math": Integration by Parts.

Instructor: Junbang LIU
TA:Lifang ZHENG

They both have a strong accent and don’t use a microphone, speaking in a very low voice. Instructor Liu teaches and writes the points by hand on paper, then uses the projector to display his notes. However, the notes are messy and presented too quickly, and he just jots them down without explanation.

author: PlanckConstant
title: MATH1013 L1 : A good grade and easy course as long as you have taken M1/2 in DSE.
comment_content:
Content :
1: Logic and Basic set theory
2: Functions
3:Injectivity and surjectivity of functions and Elementary functions
4: Sequences, limit of a sequence and Limits of a function
5: Rules of limits and Infinite limits and limit at infinity
6: Continuity
7: Differentiability and Definition of the derivative of a function
8: Rules of differentiation and Chain rule, derivation of differentiation formulas
9: Higher-order derivatives
10: Mean value theorem and ľHôpital’s rule
11: First and second derivative tests and Convexity of functions, graph sketching
12: Antiderivatives
```````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````````
大概有一半都係DSE M1/2會教嘅嘢，不過佢個approach比較偏向proof type questions，但整體學嘅嘢唔係好難，如果m2搞得掂嘅話我相信呢個course唔會搞唔掂。

For 冇學過M1 M2 嘅人就要認真啲上堂，必要時睇下herman yeung calculus 個playlist，雖然佢有時講啲嘢唔係好嚴謹，不過如果掌握唔到點樣做題目 又或者 上堂完全聽唔明嘅話睇佢啲片會幾有用。多啲睇返 功課/Midterm 錯咗啲乜 唔好重複錯 , 呢個cources careless 扣分扣得勁金。

唔好淨係做required questions ，考試有機會會翻炒problem sets，平時多啲落手做 + 考試前溫返problem sets 咁考試就唔會嚇親，上堂好少教你點樣solve exam type questions。(p.s 淨係上過一兩堂)。


CHOOSE L1 or NON L1 :
雖然L1 叫做math major section ，但係我覺得就算唔想讀MATH major 都可以揀L1，non L1 考試 時間壓力會大啲同埋冇得用計數機，再加上啲嘢簡單個個都好高分 ， 變咗你容錯率就好低， 隨時心算錯兩條已經痛失A range ，個人覺得L1 會比較接近返m2嘅考試模式。
comment_teaching:
我基本上reg 之前已經學過曬所有嘢，所以冇咩點去上堂，但我覺得教得好好，會有example 同埋 講解concept，唔係照住讀powerpoint。

reg Henry !!!!!!! (雖然應該得一個math major section冇得揀)

不過我覺得重點都係練problem sets 同埋past paper，因為上堂淨係會教你concept，唔會再好似中學老師咁逐題教你然後跟住老師做就自動五星星。

(p.s 淨係上過一兩堂)
comment_grading:
做到曬上面嘅嘢嘅話我覺得A  唔會太難，A+ 要多少少時間去練 problem sets 同埋改careless，整體嚟講我覺得係好grade course，做齊嘢自然會A range。

mean 唔知點解成日出奇地低，不過唔使理mean , 呢個course absolute grading。

我final唔係好夠時間做 (考嗰陣有啲眼瞓做嘢慢咗) ，但係最後都順利A+，不過midterm 時間係more than enough。

冇attendance，不過都建議上下堂 , 喺屋企睇video 唔係好有讀大學嘅feel XDD。
comment_workload:
唯一投訴係功課有啲多，基本上係keep住畀，相對其他year 1 course workload 比較大，而且佔16% 有少少重。

不過識做嘅話應該兩三個鐘可以搞掂，唔識做就要多花啲心機。 (擺落去嘅effort 會reflect 喺你exam scores 嗰度，所以唔會浪費時間)

author: Jason Wong ._.
comment_content:
Similar to DSE M2 calculus part, but instead of 3 years you only have 3 months. Good luck if you didn't study M1/M2.

Course outline (according to Henry's teaching):

Ch.0 Preliminaries (Basic set theory, inequality, absolute values)

Ch.1 Functions

Ch.2 Limit and continuity

Ch.3 Derivatives

Ch.4 Applications of Derivatives

Ch.5 Integrals: an informal introduction
comment_teaching:
Hu's lectures are for sleeping as for some reason he always dims the light of the lecture theater.

For proper teaching, just go to Henry's lectures (lecture recordings are provided for his lectures). Btw the A is for him, not anyone else.
comment_grading:
WeBWorK assignments (10%)
Midterm Test (35%)
Final Exam (55%)

No attendances taken for both lectures and tutorials.
comment_workload: Relatively light workload (if you already learnt calculus, ofc). Just some WebWork homework with unlimited trials. Just remember to work on them before the deadlines.

author: SnowyMelts
title: Only thing new is the questions
comment_content:
The calculus isn't the killer, it's the trigonometry. The class doesn't introduce too many new concepts when compared to the Calculus you probably studied in upper-highschool. Most of the difficulty comes from the questions you have to do. I'd recommend familiarizing yourself with trigonometry before starting this class.

Topics Covered :

Sets, Intervals, Inequalities
Limits
Continuity
Squeeze Theorem
Definition of a Derivative
Derivation Rules
Implicit, Logarithmic, Exponential Derivation
Rates of Change
Related Rates
Deriving Shapes of a Graph / Graphing Functions
Linear Approximations
Newton's Method
Absolute and Relative Maxima/Minima
Concavity and Inflection Points (2nd Derivative Tests)
Optimization
L'Hoptial's Rule
Mean Value Theorem
Definite and Indefinite Integration
Substitution Rule
Integration by Parts
Net Change
comment_teaching:
Lectures were pretty monotonous, though the teacher's explanations were pretty clear overall. The main problem is that what we get taught in classes is simpler than the questions we have to do during the exams and homework, so it feels like even more of a slog. Most people who showed up did homework during them. This is made worse by the fact that they spend a ridiculous amount of time on really basic concepts and then speedrun the actually challenging topics and questions a few weeks before the exam.

If you're really disciplined you could probably just self-study from Stewart's calculus book and do practice questions from each class' canvas page +Classviva and be fine. Especially if you were good at Calculus at the Advanced Level (or whatever they call 12th grade onwards in your country). I'd still recommend attending tutorials and going to a lecture at least somewhat occasionally so you can know what you need to focus on in your practice/studying.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading, make of that what you will.

The mean for the midterm was around 63%. For exams, you get full credit for some questions if and only if you give a brief explanation of each step you took (the questions will indicate whether or not you have to do this).
comment_workload:
9 Homework assignments on WebWork. WebWork does realtime grading, so there's really no excuse for getting a bad grade for homework. You get 2 weeks to do each assignment with around 13-14 questions (normally as many times as you want to), and between the Stewart book, Classviva (which is just the Stewart book's exercises with explanations attached) and the Math Support Center, 2 weeks is more than enough time to get 100% on each assignment.

My main complaint is that WebWork questions slightly differ from the questions we get on the examination. You can't take a calculator into the exam, but some questions rely on granular calculations. It's pretty obvious that the questions weren't tailor made for the course. I'd suggest using past final and midterm exams to study.

Tutorials are even worse, I cant even hear him since he has the mask on. The questions are difficult so if you want to have a little bit of challenge I guess you can go.
comment_grading:
Grading is dogshit that you might as well kill me. If you are a core student you cannot survive. These guys know every bit of math.

Assignments are very linear, you can definitely get full mark for it if you are not retarded and not submit it. You get unlimited retries and it helps with consolidating your knowledge.

exams should be okay for M1 M2 students. The length is not very long and you get a lot of past papers to work on (which is almost identical to the new ones that you will be taking).

author: Benson Wong
title: Henry I love you
comment_content:
There are some different between L1 and non-L1 session since L1 is designed for Science school student who are interested in major in Maths. I am an engineering student so it is not necessary for me. My science school friends regret getting into science school due to L1(may God bless them). I have learnt M2 in DSE so I don't think the content are very hard, lots of things I have learnt or seem before.
Chapter 0 Preliminaries
Set, Intervals, Inequalities, Absolute Value, Rationalization
Chapter 1 Functions
Domain, Codomain, Range, Graphs, Arithmetic operations on functions, Piecewise defined functions, Transformations of graphs of functions, Properties of functions, The inverse of a one-to-one function and its graph, Graphs and properties of elementary functions
Chapter 2 Limits and continuity
Definition of limits, One-sided limits, Operations on limits, Squeeze Theorem, Limits at infinity, Infinite limits, Asymptotes, Definition of continuity, Intermediate Value Theorem, Extreme Value Theorem, Absolute extrema and relative extrema of a function
Chapter 3 Derivatives
Definition of the derivative, Definition of differentiability, One-sided derivatives, Rules of differentiation, Derivatives of elementary functions, Techniques of differentiation, Differentiability of piecewise defined functions
Chapter 4 Applications of derivatives
Rate of change, Linear approximation, Differentials, Newton’s method, ľHôpital’s rule (the most perfect thing that I have seem in my life), Absolute extrema, local extrema and critical numbers of a function, Fermat’s Theorem about “interior” local extrema and critical numbers, Rolle’s Theorem, Mean Value Theorem for derivatives, First derivative test and second derivative test, optimization problems, Concavity of the graph of a function, points of inflection, Sketching of the graph
Chapter 5 Integrals: an informal introduction
Integral and evaluate integral, Properties of integrals, Newton-Leibniz formula, Antidifferentiation, Rules of antidifferentiation and integration, Techniques of antidifferentiation and integration, Differential equations of the form 𝑑𝑦/𝑑𝑥 = 𝑓(𝑥), initial value problems, Interpret integrals as physical quantities, Apply integration in computing the net change of a physical quantity given its rate of change
comment_teaching:
I think Henry is the best lecturer of MATH1013. His English accent is good that all students can understand. He is local and his putonghua is good too. So I don't think there will be communication problem. He is willing to answer students question and very fast to reply students email. The notes and problem sets are the most important things!!! If you don't use these notes and problem sets, you gg. Henry is the best. If you are not his student, you should use his notes and problem sets too. He is the god, Aman!
About the TA, my TA is Burton Wong and he is a good guy. He provides extra problem sets and zoom recording. So if you don't go to tutorial, it is still okay. He is willing to answer to student question. He opened discord group for student to discuss questions.

author: abc1245
title: basically m2 math in dse
comment_content: If you studied M2 in secondary school, this course might feel more manageable right from the start, since the first few chapters mainly revisit differentiation, curve sketching, and related fundamentals—stuff you’ve already tackled. Things stay familiar until you hit linear approximation; that’s where the real challenges kick in and the pace picks up. But if you never took M2 before, expect a steeper learning curve and a tougher time keeping up with the early material. Either way, make it a priority to connect with classmates at UST who have that M2 background—they’ll be gold for study groups, quick clarifications, and surviving the jump to the harder topics!
comment_teaching: Most of the professors teaching this course are from mainland China, so you might encounter some with fairly heavy accents, especially if English isn’t their primary teaching language. It can take a week or two to get used to the pronunciation and pacing, but once you do, the content usually comes through clearly.
comment_grading: Since this is a common core course for both Engineering and Science students, the grading is notoriously competitive—everyone’s gunning for those top spots, and the curve is brutal. I managed 80% on the midterm and a solid 90% on the final exam, but even that only landed me a B+ in the end. It just goes to show how high the bar is set; you really need to be near-perfect across the board to pull an A.
comment_workload: This course only allocates 10% of the final grade to homework, even though you have to slog through around 10 challenging exercises just to secure that small portion—making it feel like a ton of effort for minimal reward. That said, I think it's still relatively better than those other nightmare courses where the workload is insane and the teaching is a complete disaster.

author: Kendrickli
comment_content: If you don't need to proof this course should be easy for most students who had taken IB math or AP calculus ab. However the majority of weighting in the midterm exam consists of proofs which increases the difficulty drastically.
comment_teaching: Dr. Henry Cheng explains the content very well and is fluent in speaking English. Everyone should be able to understand his lectures pretty well.
comment_grading: Absolute grading= GPA killer
comment_workload: The workload is not very high with only 1 Webwork assignment every week. (1-2 hours) However your answer must be in the exact format Webwork wants which could waste unecessary time.

author: MTR553891
title: A Harder DSE M2 Course
comment_content:
Most of the contents are very similar to DSE M2, so if u study M1 in DSE, I strongly suggest u to study trigonometric differentiation and integration as they are not included in syllabus of M1. It will be hard for M1 students to study this course.
(I don’t have such problem as I study M2 in DSE)
comment_teaching:
Henry Cheng prepared well organised notes for us. He is able to explain everything in the slides and lecture clearly. If you still feel confused after the lecture, just go ahead and ask him questions after lecture. He is able to answer your questions in a very simple way (深入淺出，一點即明).

Sadly Henry don’t teach MATH1014 regular sessions :(
comment_grading:
In the past, most of the mid-term and final exam papers are prepared by Dr. Lam Tsz Kin (or LTK for short). If Henry prepares the exam papers, then don’t expect the style of the paper will be similar to that of the past papers. Henry’s style is totally different from that of LTK’s. So if you know that Henry is the course coordinator and he will be in charge of preparing the exam papers, find the past papers made by Henry only will be helpful.
The mid-term exam is relatively easy, but the final exam is ultra hard. However, as the exam is ultra hard, everyone get relatively low marks and thus I get a B-. :)
Friendly reminder: You are NOT allowed to use calculators in mid-term and final exam so for those who always use calculator, you need to use tons of rough work sheet during the exams. Also remember to recite the values of special angles in trigonometric functions (e.g. sin 90°=1, sin 30°=0.5, etc).
comment_workload: 9 homework sets. The deadline of the next homework=1 week after the deadline of the previous homework. Homework 1 is just a review of DSE M2 so it is easy. At the 1st week, about 2-3 homework sets are released and the deadline of 1st homework set is at about week 3 (I forgot already, but should be around this week). I strongly suggest you to try to complete all homework sets once they are released else you don’t have much time to solve them.

author: AnsonWPH
comment_content: The content is easy to follow. Since the pre-requisite of the course is lifted, MATH 1013 now can be taken by people without M2. As such, the instructor only assumes some basic-level knowledge from students, like factorization and only two trigonometric identities. Otherwise, the content is just standard calculus, from functions, limits to differentiation and integration.
comment_teaching: Professor Henry's teaching is easy to follow. He uses simple English along with memes and analogies to keep the class engaged. He also demonstrates problems on paper, which makes solving problems easier to grasp.
comment_grading:
10% - 9 WeBWorK assignments
35% - Midterm test
55% - Final exam
comment_workload: 9 WeBWorK assignments, a midterm, and a final exam. There are also suggested textbook questions and extra problem sets by Henry. If you want to get a good grade, you definitely have to put in the grind (as is for every math course).

author: Nick10086
title: Henry is my god
comment_content: L1 is basically a special version of 1013 for math major students to take; a bit more advanced material is covered compared with other regular sections. Instead of doing simple calculations all the time, L01 requires students' ability to write simple proofs.
comment_teaching: Henry is such a nice guy; he explains everything in detail with a clear accent. He can actually speak Cantonese, Mandarin, and English, so no worries if you have missed some parts during his lecture; feel free to ask him. One interesting thing about his PPT is that he usually puts some memes in it, so it was a really pleasing experience to take his lecture.
comment_grading:
I think the grading is quite generous.
The midterm mean was around 53, and the final mean was around 43.
my score: 85.5 for midterm and 59 for the final.
final score: A
One thing I'd like to note is that the midterm is much easier than the final; you can tell from the mean score. The questions in the midterm share a very similar pattern with the past paper, whereas you are not going to see this in the final exam.
comment_workload: For me, the workload was totally acceptable. One assignment with around 7 problems every 1.5 weeks. However, I do have friends who felt it was a bit difficult to solve all the assignment problems since you have to write proofs, so I would say the workload pretty much depends on your mathematical skill. Some of the questions are a bit tricky and could take you half an hour to work out the solution. But trust me, what you have put into the assignment will be totally reflected in your exam scores since the question types are very alike.

grade:B-

author: Martina_
comment_content:
No attendance requirement. I only attended the first lecture.
Went through the syllabus once or twice but it doesn't matter.
James Stweart Calculus is used. Detailed book with a huge amount of examples (and problem sets). Don't need to go through all of them, and most problem sets in the book exceed the requirement of exams (if you only want to get good grades).
Overall, appropriate difficulty to learn calculus from scratch.
comment_teaching:
Instructors don't matter, the number of monstrous mainland students in your lecture does.

I highly recommend that you:
1. Read James Stweart Calculus 7ed (this course covers part of the book).
2. Finish your homework ASAP, get extra problem sets if you're not confident in a specific chapter.
3. Get yourself prepared for the test by doing pastpaper.
4. MOST IMPORTANT: Know what you're doing. Know how and WHY you're carrying out the calculation in a particular way instead of another.
comment_grading:
A range guarantee if you:
1. Get all of your homework right.
2. No stupid mistakes in midterm and final (e.g. calculation, clerical error), as little points would be given if you don't get the answer correct in the first place.
3. You're lucky enough not to be in a lecture with all crazy students, as they'll break the curve.
REMEMBER: Little tolerance for stupid mistakes.
comment_workload:
If you've learned some calculus before, almost no workload. Main focus is placed on USING calculus to solve problems that you've already encountered (if you read the textbook carefully) rather than PROVING.

However, if you want to get A+, you have to get yourself familiar with all those proving stuff.

author: szc
title: sum
comment_content:
Calculus covers half of the topics from DSE Mathematics Extended Module M2 and introduces new concepts as well. If you're only familiar with M1, good luck!

Here’s a list (not exhaustive) of the non-M2 topics included in this course:

Continuity
Differentiability
Intermediate Value Theorem
Mean Value Theorem
One-to-one functions
Solving problems involving absolute value functions
Sandwich (Squeeze) Theorem
Linear Approximation
Newton's Method
Critical Points
Rolle's Theorem
L'Hôpital's Rule
Riemann Sums
Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (with some additional topics compared to DSE M2)
comment_teaching:
Dr. Lam has clear English and is always willing to answer student questions, which is great. However, his writing on the whiteboard can be a bit disorganized, making it challenging to follow along. The teaching pace is quite slow, with a significant amount of time spent on fundamental concepts that should have already been covered in M1, M2, and even DSE Mathematics core, particularly topics like functions. I believe more focus should be given to new material, such as L'Hôpital's Rule and other advanced concepts.

On the other hand, Mr. Kong's tutorials are more effective. He clearly demonstrates examples and highlights important steps and tips for solving problems, which is very helpful.

Overall, I find attending tutorials to be more beneficial than lectures. I tend to skip most of the lectures that focus on M2 content.

comment_workload:
There are 9-10 WebWork assignments, and you can’t simply copy from others since the numbers vary for each student, though the solving methods remain the same. To excel, build on your DSE M2 knowledge by completing tutorial questions and additional exercises from the textbook.

Past papers are widely available, so there’s no shortage of practice material—it’s up to you to make use of them. The time required for assignments depends on the complexity of the questions and your familiarity with the material, but the workload is structured to be manageable. Weekly WebWork assignments are crucial for reinforcing concepts, offering ample practice opportunities. With effective time management and strategic use of unlimited attempts, you can master the material and perform well.

author: DC22
title: 爛grade
comment_content:
Riemann sums and limit can be a bit annoying
Week 1: Review of numbers, inequalities and absolute values, functions. (Often covering Appendix A; Sections 1.1-1.3 from common textbooks.)

Week 2: Exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithms. (Sections 1.4-1.5)

Week 3: Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws. (Sections 2.1-2.4)

Week 4: Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes. (Sections 2.5-2.6)

Week 5: Derivatives and rates of change, basic derivatives, product and quotient rules. (Sections 2.7-2.8, 3.1-3.2)

Week 6: Derivatives of (inverse) trigonometric and logarithmic functions, chain rule, implicit differentiation. (Sections 3.2-3.6)

Week 7: Rates of change problems, related rates. (Sections 3.8-3.9)

Week 8: Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values. (Sections 3.10-3.11, 4.1)

Week 9: Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and the shape of a graph, L'Hôpital's rule. (Sections 4.2-4.4)

Week 10: Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s method. (Sections 4.5-4.8)

Week 11: Anti-derivatives, areas and distances, definite integrals. (Sections 4.9, 5.1-5.2)

Week 12: The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, average value of a function. (Section 5.3)

Week 13: Applications of integrals, substitution rule. (Sections 5.4-5.5)
comment_teaching: prefer self-study
comment_grading:
harshly based on an absolute grading system
85% for an A- or above
70% for a B- or above
around 50% C

comment_workload: Attendance is not taken, and lecture recordings are usually available. However, for M1 students, the course requires a significant amount of self-study and practice beyond just lectures and assignments.

author: rH_oi
title: Exam paper is too decisive
comment_content:
Format for the lesson: 3 hours of lectures and 50 mins of tutorial per week. But still, it's a science course, so not much people would attend lectures. (Only the first day the lecture hall is full)

Syllabus:
- Basic maths (Intervals, Set notations, Absolute values)
- Functions and transformations
- Trigo functions (and inverse)
- Limits and Continuity
- Rate of change, derivatives
- Exponential growth and decay
- Linear approximations, Max/Min, Mean Value Theorem
- L'Hopital's Rule
- Curve sketching, Optimization
- Definite/Indefinte Integrals

Reminder: Integrals will be covered in depth in MATH1014, so do not need to worry about the difficulty of integrals in the final exam.

No attendance requirements, just take the lessons according to your own needs.
comment_teaching:
The professor is from the mainland, but her English is acceptable even with an accent. Friendly in general and will help if you raise questions.

TA Mr Tong Kam Hung is extremely nice in tutorials. 8-10 questions are provided and detailed solution is provided after 15-20 mins of doing the questions. The questions in tutorials are useful for the midterms.
comment_grading:
Grading: From UST student's understanding, 85% or above is A range, 70% above is B range.

10% of 9-10 assignments on WebWork (remember to check it from time to time and check when the hw is due).

35% from midterms, 55% finals. Format is more or less the same, MCs and Structured questions.
The midterms was awfully easy, so the mean was above 70%, close to 80%. Finals was dreadful, LQs were hard and the mean went back down to 55%. The overall cutoff was indeed lower than the original 85% for A range, but do always assume the grades are distributed in such a way.
comment_workload:
9-10 assignments on WebWork, don't think that you can copy directly from others, cuz the questions do not have the same set of numbers (but the way to work out is completely the same).

If you want to excel in the course, besides based on your DSE M2 knowledge, do complete the questions from the tutorial, or get questions from the book and complete it.

Past papers are all over the place, so there isnt a worry that there's no stuff to work on. It is your decision to work on those or not.

title: Not that horrible even if you are non-M2 student(For 1013L1)
comment_content: Calculus I serves as the gateway to understanding change and motion, bridging abstract mathematics with real-world phenomena. The core concepts—limits, derivatives, and integrals—form the foundation for analyzing functions, rates of change, and accumulation. Derivatives model instantaneous behavior, such as velocity or growth rates, while integrals quantify total change, like area under a curve or accumulated distance. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus elegantly unites these ideas, revealing the inverse relationship between differentiation and integration. Though initially challenging, mastering these tools empowers problem-solving in physics, engineering, and economics. Calculus I isn’t just about equations; it’s a language to decode the dynamic patterns shaping our world. Embrace the rigor—it’s worth the “limit”

author: 王稀汁
title: L1 Section
comment_content:
The L1 section focus on proofing something using the theorems taught in the lectures, there's not many numbers but a lot of unknown variables and unknown functions.
The L1 section consisted of less computation, more logics and math theorems.
There are some topics that non-L1 section wouldn't include/ only briefly introduce:
> logic, set theory, proof writing
> sequences, limit of sequences (sequential limit theorem)
> rigorous proofing in limit (epsilon-delta) in midterm and final
> lot of proofing with mean value theorem (very powerful)

Also, since there are more theoretical stuffs, and the syllabus cannot include too much content, integration is not included in the syllabus (but there are antiderivatives included at the end of the course and final)
comment_teaching: Prof. Henry's teaching was quite well, ideas were fluently connected and the language he spoken was clear and easy to understand.
comment_grading:
As the mean of score of the course is around 50, if you can get above 65 in midterm and final, and your assignments has all done, you probably in the A range.
(maybe it is not difficult for those students who both get 5 or above in dse math and m1/m2, and I had skipped for many lectures)

Assignment (20%): 84%
Mid-term (40%): 74.5/100
Final (40%): 70/100
Grade: A
comment_workload: One assignment per 1.5-week, around 8 selected questions in a question set. Pretty easy to do in the first few weeks and more difficult in the later part of the semester but still affordable, at least you are able to finish all of the questions (maybe).

author: EthanKam
comment_content: The main content of this course is just talking about how to do differentiation.
comment_teaching:
which is reassuring for students who may struggle with the material. You don’t need to worry about falling behind in class; however, understanding the concepts during lectures doesn't always translate to being able to solve problems independently.

Professor Ku's hand-written notes focus on key formulae and main points but lack full explanations. While they cover the basics, they are not sufficient for preparing for the mid-term and exams. I highly recommend seeking out more organized notes for effective revision.

One notable aspect of the course structure is the lack of enforced attendance for tutorials and lectures. Although you are expected to choose a tutorial that matches your lecture number, this rule is not strictly enforced.

James Stweart Calculus is used. Detailed book with a huge amount of examples (and problem sets). Don't need to go through all of them, and most problem sets in the book exceed the requirement of exams (if you only want to get good grades).

comment_teaching: Instructors don't matter, the number of monstrous mainland students in your lecture does.

finish all the past papers and get good grades
Total score

40-50%-->D
0-40%-->Fail
comment_workload:
Homework: Basically 1 WebWork(Online homework) per week, each consists of 10-18 questions. Apart from the first one you will need 2-4 hours to finish one, not very heavy workload. You can attempt questions infinitely many times , so try your best to get full marks in them!
No attendance required for any lecture and tutorials
Exam: Do your best in the relatively simple mid-term to secure a better grade earlier. All contents will appear in final exam, so don't forget to practice on evaluating limits for the final. I focused too much on integration and didn't do a single question of limits before the final, so I lose at least 9 marks on some relatively simple limit MCs.

author: kaya_km
comment_content: If you have credit transfers, use them. Content is more in depth, but also kind of pointlessly-in-depth. If you can avoid taking the course, do that. The content is your basic calculus, but with a more annoying parts: functions, derivatives, integration, limits, etc.
comment_teaching: Nah, NEVER take him if you can. When I had a question about homework, I walked up to him after the lecture, and he literally straight up told me "you should've listened to the lecture, I won't explain you anything." Liked god, your explanation sucked, that's why I'm asking. Also, bad explanations.
comment_grading: Do not underestimate the course. Actually try solving stuff before you go into the exam room, because overestimating my abilities is what doomed me. Grading is fixed, almost no curving. Also, grading of the finals and midterms are done by TAs, and they sometimes mark your work for less than it is. Also, in the written part of the exam, DO NOT use any methods that were not taught you in the course - you will be marked down, even if you have the correct answer.
comment_workload: Only about 10 or so homeworks, very easy. You can and should go to the Math Support Centre, it is a great opportunity to get some more understanding. Most of the mentors there are great. Also, try to go in the morning - almost no one is there, so you have the whole Centre for yourself and other students who decided to get some help at this early hour. Regarding the exams: Midterm, Final, the usual. Try to make more effort, during the course. Should be relatively easy.

author: Aniketh
title: Harder than expected
comment_content:
As per website:
Week 01: 1.1 Review of functions, 1.2 Representing functions, 1.3 Inverse, exponential and logarithmic functions

Week 02: 1.4 Trigonometric functions and their inverses, 2.1 The idea of limits, 2.2 Definitions of limits

Week 03: 2.3 Techniques for computing limits, 2.4 Infinite limits, 2.5 Limits at infinity, 2.6 Continuity

Week 04: 3.1 Introducing the derivatives, 3.2 Rules of differentiation

Week 05: 3.2 Rules of differentiation, 3.3 The product and quotient rules, 3.4 Derivatives of trigonometric functions, 3.5 Derivatives as rates of change, 3.6 The chain rule

Week 06: 3.7 Implicit differentiation, 3.8 Derivatives of logarithmic and exponential functions, 3.9 Derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions

Week 07: 3.10 Related rates, 4.1 Maxima and minima

Week 08: 4.2 What derivatives tell us, 4.3 Graphing functions, 4.4 Optimization problems

Week 09: 4.5 Linear approximation and differentials, 4.6 Mean value theorem, 4.7 L'Hopital's rule

Week 10: 4.8 Newton's method (*), 4.9 Antiderivatives

Week 11: 5.1 Approximating areas under curves, 5.2 Definite integrals

Week 12: 5.3 Fundamental theorem of calculus, 5.4 Working with integrals

Week 13: 5.5 Substitution rule, Review (if there is time available)
comment_teaching: Classes aim at practice problems but are not too helpful. Could have been so much better and interactive but became tedious at some point
comment_grading: Better than expected.....Originally expected a D ended up with a C+ which is much better. The midterms are not too hard but the final exam is killer. Exam paper was not remotely related to what we learnt during the course
comment_workload: Low.....Not too much, just have to finish assignments every week. Prepare for finals and midterms much before exam

title: DO NOT UNDERESTIMATE!
comment_content:
Covers Chapter 1-5 from Calculus Early Transcendentals by James Stewart. (You can get PDF from online, ez).

Exam questions are mind boggling. Yes the content is similar to A Level Pure Maths, but the questions that will appear in the exams will require so much more critical thinking than regular A Level questions.
comment_teaching:
Some teachers are not quite good, others are really good. Henry Cheng is strongly recommended. He provides neat and complete summary of topics in the form of PPT and provides lots of practice sets.

Tbh my lecturer was not too good. Looking back, I think doing self study from just the Textbook would do more good lmao.
comment_grading:
Fixed grading. Sounds bad but if you get a bad grade, the fixed grading system will save you.

comment_workload: 10 short assignments, attendance not taken. But you have to do lots of practice questions, especially from past paper. I recommend practicing from ClassViva (a website, accessible by all HKUST students) and also the transcendental book if you have.
