comment_teaching: Herry's teaching is enjoyable because he taught everything clearly with deep insights. For almost every complex conclusion or equation, he derived it and do made the logic clear rather than just asking students to memorize it. He did a good job combining his ppt and handwriting. His lecture notes are well-designed and you can easily follow him by reading the notes and writing down the solutions to the questions.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading(with some adjustments according to the difficulties of the exams.

Midterm Test (35%)
Final Exam (55%)
WeBWorK assignments (10%):
WeBWorK homework sets will be assigned from time to time and (most of them are similar questions taught in class
 !!!!!Extra problem sets (0%):
Henry made these problem sets for his students(but students from other lessons can also find them on Canvas). Although extra problem sets are not counted towards the final grade, you are highly encouraged to work out the solutions to the problems because it's really helpful for midterm and final exams.
The mid-term and final exams will normally be closed-book written tests, and calculators will not be allowed during the tests.
Past papers are very important and you are highly suggested to do them to prepare for the exams. I got an A+ this year just because I did all the past papers I could find.
comment_workload: The workload is acceptable once you understand everything the professor teaches in class.

author: MyNameIsKhan708
comment_content: One of the toughest courses in HKUST. The content seems simplistic and easy to follow, with limits and continuity comprising of a majority of the course. However, the questions provided in the final exam require a lot of tricky thinking, and a simple misunderstanding could mess up your entire paper.
comment_teaching: If you talk to him nicely, Mr Chiang is a very good professor. However, I must say it is tough to follow up with the course content as he speaks.
comment_grading: The grading is extremely non-leniant. You have to get over upper quartile to secure a B grade. I think mean is usually 60, and getting 85 is an A-. Remember, there are very few people who get an A range, so to be one of those, you should have consistent effort. I got 66 on midterm and the mean was only 64, I got lower on final, yet managed to attain a B grade.
comment_workload: The workload is all dependant on you. If you would like to practice more, you would have a high workload. But the important content does not require heavy workload, and it is certainly possible to cover everything in just a month, if a person wants that.

author: SAM0506
title: Math 1013
comment_content: This is an introductory course in one-variable Calculus, the first in the Calculus I and II sequence, designed for students that have taken HKDSE Mathematics Extended Module M1/M2. Topics include functions and their limits, continuity, derivatives and rules of differentiation, applications of derivatives, and basic integral calculus.
comment_teaching: Mr Henry Cheng is the best instructor that I have meet. During his lecture, he sometimes poses some funny meme picture of Mathematics to make us fun. Also, he is willing to answer the questions of students. Moreover, his teaching is clear so that I can understand the concepts easily. Because of him, I love this course and his lecture very much!!!!!
comment_grading:
10%:Homework from the Webwork platform
35%:Mid-term
55%:Final
First, for homework,in order to get the 10% easy mark, you must complete all weekly homework in the Webwork. If you have any questions on it, you can go to the Math Support Center for assistance.
Second, for the mid-term, it is much easier than the final. So, it is a chance for you to get as higher score as you can so as to guarantee a passing grade.
Third, for the final, aka the big boss, it is much harder than the mid-term. So, you have to pay much attention to it.
If your final score is near the mean, you will have higher probability to get the grade of B-/C+
comment_workload:
It is a fair course. The more effort you put on the course, the higher grade you will get. In order to get a good grade, you must work harder than the other and  I would suggest you to start revision two weeks before the mid-term and one month before the final.
Good luck and good health for everyone

author: gaiaforce
title: DSE M2 > DSE M1
comment_content:
1. Review of numbers, inequalities and absolute values, functions
2. Exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithms
3. Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws
4. Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes
5. Derivatives and rates of change, basic derivatives, product and quotient rules
6. Derivatives of (inverse) trigonometric and logarithmic functions, chain rule, implicit differentiation
7. Rates of change problems, related rates
[ Mid-term ]
8. Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values
9. Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and the shape of a graph, L'Hopital's rule
10. Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton's method
11. Anti-derivatives, areas and distances, definite integrals
12. The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, average value of a function
13. Applications of integrals, substitution rule
[ Final Exam ]

Most of the content is covered in DSE M2, and really, you can handle the mid-term well if you are good at M2. I think DSE M1 cover less than M2 but it wouldn't be too hard to learn anyway. For final, ahem, hellish so glhf :)

author: xX_Student_Xx
comment_content:
B

While most of the big topics (limit, differentiation, integration) are covered in M2 DSE, this course includes more details and applications.
*: not covered by M2 DSE
Syllabus: *(IIRC, i think it may have been in core) interval notation, *absolute value, limit, *1-sided limit (a->0+ or a->0-), *squeeze theorem, *continuity, *intermediate value theorem, *extreme value theorem, differentiation and the rules, *differentiability, *linear approximation, *newton's method, *l'ho(s)pital rule, *mean value theorem, curve sketching, optimization, antiderivative, *riemann sum, *(IIRC) fundamental theorem of calculus

(Only applies to M2 takers, I don't know the syllabus of M1) The syllabus looks fine as a must-take for freshmen, as it covers the already-known topics along with some extra subtopics. This makes the course less demanding, hence, more suited for new students adapting to university, unlike a certain freshman course course (looking at you, phys1112).

The extra problem sets are also useful as it contains some exam-like questions and for reinforcing concepts. However, even though they are accessable for everyone, I agree that it is a bit fucked up that only Henry's sessions know about the sets (except for L3/L9 as the sets are pasted there), which may give an unfair advantage for students in his session.

Another thing to nitpick is that the provided midterm and final pastpapers are from around 2017-18, which may have contributed to the wide gap of the question types (especially MC) between the given pps and this year's final, misleading students.
comment_teaching:
Henry Cheng: A

A very effective instructor.

WARNING: yapping

Let's admit it, maths is a relatively boring subject: one can watch interesting demos/illustrations in phy/chem/bi(o), one can read/watch comedies in humanities/language. However, there is not much entertainments or interactions when it comes to maths, besides problems and exercises.

This is where Henry shines. When introducing topics, he often includes details related to them: from memes (funny or not) attached to the slides, to stories like how l'Ho(s)pital "stole" Bernoulli's works. While not completely eliminating the dullness of maths, he makes his lectures more entertaining.

Some may ask: Being entertaining is merely a factor of teaching. How about delivering concepts to students, as the duty of a lecturer? He also got you covered!

He clearly explains topics by not only going through what the topic is, but also common misconceptions surrounding it. E.g. invalidity of |tanb-tana|≤|b-a| due to tan being discontinuous, flaw of using l'Ho(s)pital rule for equations like lim(x->+inf) (e^x+sinx)/(e^x+cosx) (unfortunately, I still got those questions wrong in the final). On top of that, he does examples of topics by writing on a piece of paper, clearly projected on a screen. This makes it easy to follow through the steps.

Alas, he won't be teaching non-L1 for Math1014 from what I saw in the preview canvas page. I will miss his teaching.
comment_grading:
C+->B

Ez midterm but brutal final.

total = 10% webwork + (0% problem set) + 35% midterm + 55% final

Webwork: full

Histogram for midterm and final are attached.
Midterm:
Mean = 67.31; S.D. = 19.70
LQ = 54; Median = 70; UQ = 83; Max = 100
My Performance = 95 (1.4 SD)

Final:
Mean = 50.33; SD = 18.55
LQ = 36.5; Median = 49.5; UQ = 64; Max = 97.5
My performance: 88.5 (2.06 S.D.)

Total = 10%+95*35%+88.5*55%=91.925% (A+)

Criterion referencing is used, i.e. neither absolute grading nor 拉curve (norm-referencing). The grade cutoff is lower than the original 85 (A)/70 (B)/40 (D) line considering the difficulty of the final (Cheng, 2024).

In this sem, the final's MCs are way different from previous years (2017-18): there are many more concept checks, such as l'Ho(s)pital rule's limitations, when not to apply Newton's Method. This is where I lost most of my marks.
As for long questions, the format is still similar. The 3-4 questions are usually:
-1 rate of change/optimization
-1 curve-sketching
-1 solve integral/limit/derivative and proof things using x value theorem
While this year's format is still similar, the way the questions are designed makes this year's final hard (fucking optimization of cone with sphere of fixed radius inside and inequality proofs).

I cannot comment on or rate the grading in general as there's too little information, but I'd say it's not too bad for A+ competition as getting barely over 2 S.D. in the final gives me A+ so I'll settle this category on B.
comment_workload:
B for light workload

9 Webwork assignments over 13 weeks, 10+ questions each, doesn't take too long to complete, except for that fucking paper-folding optimization or optimization problems in general.

However, it is recommended to do the extra problem sets (canvas->math1013->home->the lecture taught by Henry) especially if Henry is the course coordinator, since there are some similar problems there as the ones in the final.

author: Bevos
title: More Effort is Needed
comment_content:
"Topics include functions and their limits, continuity, derivatives and rules of differentiation, applications of derivatives, and basic integral calculus."
But the truth is, this course skipped much content, such as the definition of limit, which can be found in almost every textbook of calculus. However, these knowledge is necessary to form a comprehensive understanding of calculus. I would suggest learning something beyond the course material, based on a textbook. It's also worth mentioning that although the examples in class are easy, and you may get past papers, but please pay attention to practicing. Exams may not always be that easy, just like this semester.

comment_grading: The grading is friendly. Don't panic, even if the exam is hard. As long as everyone feels difficult, you may still get a satisfying grade even if you don't get flying marks.
comment_workload: Not much workload if you only satisfy the basic needs, that is, homework. But if you want to do a bit better and develop your understanding of the subject, much effort is needed.

author: MaxZ
title: Run away from Chiang
comment_content: Math1013 covers fundamental concepts in single-variable calculus, including topics such as limits, derivatives, integrals, and the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. The course is designed to provide a solid foundation for future math courses, but many found the final exam to be unexpectedly difficult, with a heavy focus on proof-based questions that were not well-explained in class. Overall, the material is suitable for first-year students, though it can be challenging when applied in exams.
comment_teaching: Professor Chiang’s teaching style has been criticized for being ineffective. He relies heavily on whiteboard explanations without using slides or other visual aids, making it difficult for students—especially those seated in the back—to follow along. His pacing is also inconsistent: he spends too much time on basic concepts while rushing through more complex ones, leaving students confused. Many students found his lectures boring and felt they could learn the material on their own, as his class lacked engagement and interaction. Additionally, his lecture notes were disorganized, making it hard for students to effectively review the content.
comment_grading:
The grading for Math1013 is divided into Webwork (10%), the midterm (35%), and the final exam (55%). While Webwork assignments are manageable and an easy way to earn full marks, the midterm and final exams are much more challenging. The final exam, in particular, is very difficult and significantly impacts the overall grade, with many students struggling to score well. The grading system is criterion-referenced, but students have expressed dissatisfaction with the lack of transparency regarding grading adjustments and the absence of an appeals process for exam results.

Me: Overall A+

While the weekly workload is not overwhelming, the final exam demands extensive preparation, and students are advised to complete past papers and practice additional problems to succeed.

author: GUOYU
title: L02-09 Non-MATH-major sections
comment_content:
This course teaches the fundamentals of calculus (basically focuses on differentiation, but will cover integration in the last few weeks.)

Syllabus & Class arrangement:
1. Chapter 0 Preliminaries
    This chapter is just a revision of high school knowledge. No matter what background you have (International student, HKDSE or Mainland JEE), you should be very familiar with these stuff.
a) Basic set theory (sets, elements, relationship between two sets; intervals: open and close)
b) Inequalities in one unknown (solving basic inequalities)
c) Absolute values (solve inequalities with absolute values)
d) Sigma notation (here introduces some summing formulae that will be useful in later chapters -> the calculation of Riemann Sum when sub-interval tends to 0)
2. Chapter 1 Functions
    Different from MATH 1023 (the honor version), this course will start from talking about functions (not sequences).
a) Basic notions about functions: Domain, codomain, range
b) The graph of a function, “vertical line test”
c) Arithmetic operations on functions (the harder part is composition)
d) Piecewise defined functions (absolute function and general piecewise defined functions)
e) Transformations of graphs of functions (more focuses on graphs)
f) Properties of functions (Odd functions / Even functions / Periodic functions / (Strictly) increasing functions / (Strictly) decreasing functions / Bounded functions / One-to-one functions, “horizontal line test”)
g) The inverse of a one-to-one function and its graph (the inverse function will become harder when differentiation is introduced)\
h) Drawing graphs of functions
3. Chapter 2 Limits and continuity
    From this chapter you will finally 'realize' you are learning calculus. Many concepts will be introduced and the hardest part in this chapter is the calculation of limits (when I say 'hard' it does not mean its super complicated, instead, its the thing that you should pay attention. -> in exams)
a) The informal definition of limits of functions at a real number / One sided limits
b) Basic arithmetic operations on limits / Limits of elementary functions / Evaluating limits of functions using factorization or rationalization
c) Evaluating limits of functions using a change of variables
d) Squeeze Theorem
e) Basic limits
f) Limits related to functions (Limits at infinity / Asymptotes)
4. Chapter 3 Derivatives
    This chapter will teach you how to calculate derivatives using limits (not the rules and formulae) -> based on what you have learnt in previous chapter.
    The most important thing you should practice is Implicit differentiation. You will see them everywhere in Midterm and Final.
5. Chapter 4 Applications of derivatives
    In this chapter, you will learn L'Hôpital’s rule (so you are not expected to use it in the Mid-term.)
    You will use everything you learn to solve problems (optimization / physics / graph sketching etc.)
6. Chapter 5 Integrals
    In the last chapter of this course, you will learn integration (staring from the calculation of Riemann Sum).
a) To estimate areas bounded by curves using rectangular strips (Riemann sums / Error approximation)
b) To evaluate integrals from definition or using known results from geometry
c) Properties of integrals
d) Area function
e) Fundamental Theorem of Calculus in two versions (here you can use the formulae to calculate anti-derivatives)
f) To evaluate antiderivatives and integrals by substitution
comment_teaching:
Henry is the best.
By the way, his problem sets are truly useful. (Better do them before exams.)
comment_grading:
I give GRADING a B because I have a mixed feeling.
First thing you need to know is that this course is not graded on curve. Overall 85% and above means A range and 70% or above means B range. The overall score is calculated as follows:
10%    Homework
35%    Mid-term
55%    Final
* Note that the percentage of A / B / C / D range will shift according to the difficulties of the exams.

This is why I have a mixed feeling, the mid-term is SUPER easy, but if you make careless mistakes, you will looking for a better final result, however, the final exam is HARD (compared to homework / mid-term and past papers from 2017-2019). So, you will somehow lose 'hope' after final. Luckily, we have the (*) thing, the 85% line will change accordingly, so you should survive after the final.
comment_workload:
This depends on your background. Typically the workload is quite small (but you can try to increase it by finding extra practice sets and challenging long questions).
BUT, never overlook this course, PAY ATTENTION to the mid-term and final exams. Do practice regularly.

author: TR3935
title: [L1 MATH Major Section Review]
comment_content:
**This review is for L1 math major section**
This course consists of 5 chapters:
[Chapter 0: Preliminaries] Difficulty 5/10
-focuses on mathematical logic.
-logical operations, converse, contrapositive
-proof by contradiction etc.
It's not difficult at all. However, please pay attention to statements involving 'for all' and 'for some'. In exams, they may ask you to write the negation of those statements. Also, remember the format of the second principle of MI (proof for n=1 and n=2 case, assume n=k and n=k+1, then proof n=k+2 case), as it may be useful for future chapters.

[Chapter 1: Functions] Difficulty 5/10
- Domain, codomain, range
- transformation of graphs of function
- odd, even, periodic functions
- injective, surjective, and bijective functions (VERY IMPORTANT)
- Monotone squeeze thm (IMPORTANT)
- other elementary functions
The only difficult thing in this chapter is proving why the function is injective, surjective, and bijective. Also, injective functions will be cross-linked with other chapters, so pay more attention.

[Chapter 2: Limits] Difficulty 8/10
-Convergence
-Squeeze Thm (IMPORTANT)
-Monotone Squeeze Thm (IMPORTANT)
-Rigorous definition of limits (DIFFICULT)
-Continuity
-Intermediate Value Thm. (IMPORTANT), Extreme value theorem.
Proofing limits using a rigorous definition of limits is required. If you do not get the idea in the beginning, please watch youtube videos (e.g. blackpenredpen) to help you learn.

[Chapter 3: Dervatives] Difficulty 3/10
You can take a rest while learning this chapter :) Since 60-70% of stuffs are already covered in DSE M2.
Some new things but not difficult:
- differentability of piecewise defined function
- derivative of inverse
- Leibniz Rule
- linear approx. and Newton's method

[Chapter 4: Mean Value Therom (MVT)] Difficulty 7/10
This chapter covers MVT and the consequences of MVT.
First 50% of Ch4: Rolle's Theorem and Mean Value Theorem
It is challenging to write proofs using MVT. You can use graphs to help you think but your proofing should not rely on graphs.
Last 50% of Ch4: Curve Sketching and Basic Integration.
Easy, mostly DSE stuff. You are required to draw curves involving trigonometric functions, piecewise-defined functions, functions involving absolute signs etc. So pay attention to differentiability and asymptotes.

author: Christchen
comment_content: In terms of content, this course is perceived by mathematics students as one that emphasizes calculation. Previous exams have confirmed this perspective. Whether you choose L1 or L2-L11, the course is heavily focused on calculations. However, if you intend to join the mathematics department, whether it’s MATH, MAEC, or DSCT, please make an effort to choose 1013L1 or 1023! The rationale is straightforward: mathematics is fundamentally a subject centered around proofs. At least in calculus, once you enter the mathematics department, you will need to learn proofs. While it is noted that proofs are primarily taught in 2033, many students taking 2033 may have already completed 1024 and 2001 (data shows that around 140 math majors are admitted each year, with at least 30 to 40 students from 1024 achieving A grades annually). So please do not harbor any illusions that there are few proof questions in mathematics!
comment_teaching: Easy
comment_grading: As for grading, this course was previously graded using a combination of relative and absolute methods, but I’ve heard it has now shifted to absolute grading, with A grades typically ranging from 80-90 and B grades above 70. Don’t think that just passing this course is sufficient; while it is not overly difficult, it won’t benefit you in the long run.
comment_workload: Regarding workload, I have heard some details about grading: in 1013L1, the A range is approximately 10%-20%, while the F range is around 30%. For 1014L2-L11 and L1, the distribution of grades in spring 2022 was approximately 16% in the A range, 32% in the B range, 38% in the C range, 7% in the D range, and 6% in the F range. I hope this information proves helpful to you.

comment_content:

In terms of content, this course is actually a subject that emphasizes calculation in the eyes of mathematics students. Of course, the examination papers in previous years have also proved this point. This course, whether L1 or L2-L11, is very calculation-oriented. But if you want to choose the mathematics department, whether it is MATH, MAEC or DSCT, please try to choose 1013L1 or 1023! The reason is very simple. In fact, mathematics is a subject that focuses on proofs. At least in calculus, you still have to learn proofs after entering the mathematics department. Although it is said that proofs are learned in 2033, in fact, many people who study 2033 may have studied 1024 and 2001 (data: about 140 math majors are admitted each year, and at least 30 to 40 1024 students get A range each year). Please do not have any illusions about mathematics that there are little proof questions! ! !

author: Emz28
comment_content:
Week one:
Functions, their representations. (1.1)
Some basic functions. (1.2)
Exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithms (1.4, 1.5)
New functions from old functions (composition) (1.3)

Week two:
Trigonometric functions. (appendix D)
Inverse trigonometric functions (appendix D)
Tangent and velocity. (2.1)

Week three:
Definition of limit (2.2)
Basic rules for limit calculations. (2.3)
Continuity. (2.5)

Week four:
Limit at infinity, and horizontal asymptotes. (2.6)
Derivatives and rates of change. (2.7)
Basic Derivatives. (2.8, 3.1)

Week five:
Differentiation rules, product, quotient. (3.2)
Derivatives of trigonometic functions. (3.3)
Chain rule. (3.4)

Week six:
Implicit differentiation. (3.5)
Derivatives of logarithmic functions, and inverse trignometric functions. (3.6)

Week seven:
Rate change problems, and exponential growth. (3.7, 3.8)
Related Rates. (3.9)

Week eight:
Linear approximations, and differentials. (3.10)
Newton's method. (4.8)
Applications, maxima, minima. (4.1)

Week nine:
Mean value theorem. (4.2)
Derivatives and the shape of a graph. (4.3)
L'Hopital's rule. (4.4)

Week ten:
Curve sketching. (4.5, 4.6)
Optimization problems. (4.7)

Week eleven:
Anti-derivatives. (4.9)
Areas and distances. (5.1)

Week twelve:
Definite integrals. (5.2)
The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus. (5.3)

Week thirteen:
Indefinite integrals and net change. (5.4)
Substitution. (5.5)

Midterm 大多數都係M2嘢為主，D數同簡單嘅In 數
final 有一半都係新嘢

author: maxchoi
title: mid-term 前後兩個世界
comment_content: math 1012/13/14都係作為後a-level年代後，連接返form4-6到ug level嘅course，所以天生定位比較似a-level中嘅pure math，難度上雖然冇辦法同pure比，但係意在比學生體驗下ug level嘅世界係點，所以content上嘅approach就同M1/2好唔同，呢啲就係中學同大學嘅分別，需要學生慢慢熟習，難度上就要分開mid-term前後，mid-term前嘅嘢就大多十分輕鬆，就算係新學嘅都好，都唔有太多嘅計算，大概係一啲function再加少少differentiation，而mid-term後難度會開始上升，differentiation入邊嘅mean-value theorem ，L'Hospital rule，開始會有pure math嘅感覺，而之後integration會學riemann sum，fundamental theorem of calculus，呢到就係全course最難個度，若果你覺得都唔係太難，咁就恭喜你，安全渡過咗係ust嘅第一個sem嘅第一個course，若果你覺得有啲辛苦，咁建議多加練習，唔識就去math support centre求救
comment_teaching: 差唔多冇上過堂咁濟，所以不作評論
comment_grading:
呢個course有大量non-local，呢堆人會推到個mean好高，所以其實對於local嚟講係爛grade course嚟，先講講M1，如果你係M1出身，咁可能要自求多褔，全course絕大部份嘢都係同M1冇關，所以好大可能要不斷操數，但最後都會俾non-local (especially nds)拎晒啲A走，如果你係M2出身，咁會好好多，mid-term 前應該都唔使點溫書都有B/B+，但係mid-term後都係需要操數，唔係都會爛埋一份，而如果你係non-local而又唔知點解會睇得明呢篇文，咁你地應該係唔掂書都會有A range

呢個course嘅exam對local極度不利，因為唔可以用cal，而M2學生應該係識用cal計calculus，所以操數嘅時候，要練埋唔用cal，唔係計differentiaion或者integration冇cal用check數，容易有careless，non-local例如nds本身都冇得用cal，對佢地嚟講係一種優勢

本人係M2出身，所以都覺得還可以

mid-term:88/100
final:打文嘅時侯未出，我估70-80/100掛

author: MmCat
comment_content:
It was divided into 6 Chapters in my section

Chapter 0 Preliminaries (Just solving inequalities, absolute value functions and sigma notations)

Chapter 1 Functions (It is just about finding domains and ranges, Harder ones include inverse functions, not a difficult chapter)

Chapter 2 Limits (M2 stuffs start to appear more + continuity, still not much stuffs that are new, just Squeeze theorem is something new)

Chapter 3 Derivatives (Just M2 stuffs)

Chapter 4 Application of derivatives, Mean value theorem, L'Hôpital's rule (Hardest chapter in my opinion, where it is basically all new stuffs after application of derivatives. You get news stuffs like Newton's method, linear approximation, mean value theorem, L'Hôpital's rule)

To be honest, those are not that difficult to use, just it will be harder if you want dig deeper and understand every single proof of those theories which is unnecessary.

Chapter 5 Integration (Medium difficulty, at first you get stuffs like Riemann's sums to know, basically you get to study the fundamental theory of integration first, then some M2 stuff like integration by substitution)

Not much integration in chapter 5 as most integration techniques and stuffs are in Math 1014 I think.

Basically the first 3 chapters are easy and you can skip all those lectures if you read his notes for real. I recommend you come back when he starts teaching chapter 4, that's when things start to get harder.
comment_teaching: Probably the best professor in Math 1013. He explains the subject very clearly. His English accent is pretty good. His notes are very clear and easy to understand, it will be easy for you to catch up even if you have skipped his lectures and just learn it once again by reading his notes. He also provides you with Problem Sets, but those are way more difficult compared to my homework (at least for me). The questions in the problem set are useful but not necessary as those questions are even harder than my mid term exam.
comment_grading:
I think it is absolute grading,
with 85% A-range

They gave us 3 past paper before midterm, but somehow those 3 papers are extremely similar so kinda pointless to do all 3. Also our midterm is definitely way different than the past papers. Anyways the past papers they gave were like in 2016,2017,2018.

Tutorials are not necessary and not useful for me at least, they will post the notes on Canvas anyways so it is just better to read the tutorial notes later if you want to.

Distribution:
10% homework
35% midterm
55% final
comment_workload: Really light workload, one homework every week. The first few ones are extremely easy. Only starting to get hard after application of derivatives. Well, it's not really hard, just time consuming at that point when you simply do optimisations and rate of change questions endlessly.

As a m2 student, you might stumble if you did not study it clear enough. For my case, i am lazy so I did not write any clear and complete steps for most of my answers during my midterm so the score is 5X% which is slightly over the mean. While for the final, 8X% after taking it seriously.
Just remember to check if there is any assignments, or else you will miss one or two just like me :)
Final score b+
comment_workload: almost every week hv new assignments but you hv unlimited chance to answer so should be able to finish easily as long as you follow samples from the slides

author: Heily
comment_content: Calculus. Half DSE-M2 stuff, half new things. Good luck if you are M1. Here's a list(not complete) of non-M2 stuffs in this course: Continuity, Differentiability, Intermediate Value Theorem, Mean Value Theorem, 'one-to-one' function, Solving problems with absolute functions, Sandwich(Squeeze) Theorem, Linear Approximation, Newton's Method, Critical Point, Rolle's Theorem, L'Hopital Rule, Reimann Sum, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus(A little more things than DSE M2)
comment_teaching: Dr. Lam's English is clear, and he is willing to answer student's questions. However, his writes on the whiteboard in a less organised way, so it's not easy to follow. The teaching pace is slow, and he put too much time on explaining the fundamental knowledge that both M1 and M2 and even DSE Maths core should have covered, especially functions... He should have put more efforts on new topics such as L'Hopital Rule and more...  Mr. Kong's tutorial is better, at least he can demonstrate the examples clearly and remind us some important steps/tips in solving problems.  I think attending tutorial is more important than attending lecture. I skip most lectures that teaches M2 stuff.
comment_grading: Semi-absolute grading. Much fairer than previous semesters. 15% WebWork 35% Mid-term 50% Final Total score  85-100%-->A range 70-85%-->B range 50-70%-->C range 40-50%-->D 0-40%-->Fail Me: WebWork Full mark Mid-term 90/100 (Mean: 73.43 SD:15.77) Final 65/100 (Mean: 63.46 SD:15.37) Total 79/100 (Mean: 71.89 SD:12.94) -->B You can see that getting a mean will give you B-, much fairer than the past.
comment_workload: Homework: Basically 1 WebWork(Online homework) per week, each consists of 10-18 questions. Apart from the first one you will need 2-4 hours to finish one, not very heavy workload. You can attempt questions infinitely many times , so try your best to get full marks in them! No attendance required for any lecture and tutorials Exam: Do your best in the relatively simple mid-term to secure a better grade earlier. All contents will appear in final exam, so don't forget to practice on evaluating limits for the final. I focused too much on integration and didn't do a single question of limits before the final, so I lose at least 9 marks on some relatively simple limit MCs, sigh

author: Tam1kosha
comment_content: The course provided a solid foundation in the core concepts of single-variable calculus. We covered limits, continuity, differentiation, and integration in great depth. The lessons on taking derivatives of various functions, using the power rule, product rule, quotient rule, and chain rule were particularly helpful. The unit on integrals, including u-substitution and integration by parts, was also very informative.
comment_teaching: Unfortunately, the professor's teaching and communication skills were subpar. Lectures were often disorganized, with the professor jumping between topics without clear explanations or transitions. Key concepts were not always explained thoroughly, leaving many students confused.
comment_grading: While the grading in this course was not perfect, it was not terrible either. The professor provided clear grading rubrics for assessments, and feedback on assignments was generally helpful. The final grades seemed to reflect a reasonable assessment of each student's understanding of the material.
comment_workload: The workload for this course was manageable, with weekly homework assignments, quizzes, and two exams. The homework problems were challenging enough to reinforce the concepts, but not overly time-consuming. The pace of the course was reasonable, allowing students to keep up with the material.

author: Zan
title: MATH1013 - Review
comment_content:
MATH1013 is a standard mathematics course that is required for engineering degrees in HKUST. The content as of Spring 23-24 covers:
Integration by Substitution, Optimization Problems, Curve Sketching, Related Rates, Implicit Differentiation, Chain Rules, Evaluation of Limits + L'Hopital's Rule, Evaluation of Simple Indefinite/Definite Integrals, Riemann Sum, Newton's Method, Differentials, Average Value of a function, Linear Approximation.

Tutor
comment_teaching:
I will grade the teaching based on separate instructors.

Prof Xiong, Maosheng, is a great lecturer. He reviews his notes in class and works on MATH1013 problems that are related and show up in the mid-term/final exam. At first, his teaching plan was quite slow and was hurried up towards the end, however I am satisfied with this level of teaching as its a standard higher than other professors.

TA - LAU, Hing Sing is the probably the best TA I have ever had in HKUST, the amount of dedication to helping, answering questions, providing solutions and exam practice for students is unheard of and he is probably the golden standard of TAs.

TA - ZHANG, Wenling, given the one hour periods we had for the specific tutorials, it wasnt very well done. I get that she seemed new and was learning how to teach properly but it was so low effort that it wasn't even worth turning up for.
comment_grading: Grading was a lot less harsh as there weren't as many students taking MATH1013 in Spring compared to Fall. Overall the grading was fair and the examinations were easy as long as you practice what the lecturer said would appear.
comment_workload:
MATH1013 is a heavy workload course. Why?

Well any form of mathematics requires a lot practice no matter what. Just do a study plan and revise early.

(For this course, the assignments were sent out late for some reason, but I guess the professor was busy, so its eh-eh)

94.75 <= A+
89.75 <= A < 94.75
79.75 <= B+ < 84.75
74.75 <= B < 79.75
69.75 <= B- < 74.75
62.75 <= C+ < 69.75
54.75 <= C < 62.75
46.75 <= C- < 54.75
37.5 <= D < 46.75
F < 37.5

comment_grading:

Midterm 35%
Final 55%

author: Jensen LO
title: 千祈唔好走堂
comment_content:
Week 1 Review of numbers, inequalities and absolute values, functions
Appendix A,B; Sections 1.1-1.3
Week 2 Exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithms
Appendix D, Sections 1.4-1.5
Week 3 Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws
Sections 2.1-2.4
Week 4 Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes
Sections 2.5-2.6
Week 5 Derivatives and rates of change, basic derivatives, product and quotient rules
Sections 2.7-2.8, 3.1-3.2
Week 6 Derivatives of (inverse) trigonometric and logarithmic functions, chain rule, implicit differentiation
Sections 3.2-3.6
Week 7 Rates of change problems, related rates
Sections 3.8-3.9
Week 8 Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values
Midterm (10/29) Sections 3.10-3.11, 4.1
Week 9 Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and the shape of a graph, L‘Hopital’s rule
Sections 4.2-4.4
Week 10 Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s method
Sections 4.5-4.8
Week 11 Anti-derivatives, areas and distances, definite integrals
Sections 4.9, 5.1-5.2
Week 12 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, average value of a function
Section 5.3
Week 13 Applications of integrals, substitution rule
Sections 5.4-5.5
comment_teaching: The teaching style... Slow pace makes ppl sleepy + The accent of the lecture makes ppl hard to catch the point.
comment_grading: The course is easy, but I suggest not skipping any classes because you might miss some important content.
comment_workload: The work load is not heavy. What you need to do is only to finish all the hw, and chur a little bit for the midterm and final.

author: Antarctica
comment_content:
Review&Function, Limit, Differentiation&application, Integral

Not difficult. Mid-term covers functions, limit, and differentiation, and final exam covers whole content

For mainland students with gaokao background (like me), the contents before mid-term are basically covered in gaokao, which is quite easy to understand. However, it still need hard work to get used to learn math under English context.

The second part, integral, is more challenging to understand, but is still ok
comment_teaching:
Prof. Tang is very patient, although she is a new teacher. And you can talk with her in Mandarin

comment_grading:
Homework 10%
Mid-term    35%
Final          55%
No attendance/other quiz

My score:
HM 100%
Mt   90/100
Final  unclear but around 92-93
Total  92.65/100, A

Grading policy is fair, your grade depends on your mastery of knowledge. Compared with gaokao (尤其是新一卷数学...）, the exams do not need too many special skills and extra knowledge, they only test whether you totally understand what you learned in class. So don't be nervous. In fact this course has successfully cured my math phobia caused by gaokao
comment_workload:
Very slight workload. The questions are easy to deal with, and we have unlimited chances to try, until you get full mark.

But only finishing homework may be not enough to handle exams, it is beneficial to do some pastpapers and extra exercises

TEACHING

Dr. CHENG is a nice lecturer, with a clear accent and very patience. He is able to speak both English, Cantonese and Putonghua. He will also use Cantonese and Putonghua to answer your questions after class.

author: HotLasagna
title: L01 Review
comment_content: Prof Cheng's lecture notes are amazing, blanks are included for you to fill in the blank in lectures. The notes basically covers all the aspects that are taught in class and highlights certain parts which are emphasized for students who aim for a higher grade. (I suggest you go through all of it)
comment_teaching: Prof Cheng's teaching is very good, as long as you attend each lecture you won't get a bad grade. Personally, prof Cheng explains many basic things in detail making the lecture very friendly to people like me who haven't studied math very throughly in high school.
comment_grading:
Grading is fairly good; I got B without much studying, skipping many lectures and never going to the tutorials. As long as you attend each lecture and do the homework I think it is not hard to get above B+.

Remember to do all the homework including the ones that are not required, as they help you the most for the exams.

Moreover, if you want to get a high grade in the exams you must not only fully understand the course material but also be required to expand further.

Overall, if you can do the homework easily by yourself, the exams should be easy for you as I think most of the homework questions are harder than the exam.
comment_workload:
The workload is suitable, but you would need to spend time after lectures to dissect what you've learned.

The homework load is not high but personally it was very difficult for me to accomplish each question. However, it gets better once you go further into the course.

author: Chan Ying Tung
title: Pretty good instructor
comment_content: MATH 1013 is an overall a advanced course. If you have a strong Mathematics background, it should not be hard for you. For example, the integration, differentiation. However, you need to attend the lesson to catch up with some newly popped concept.
comment_teaching: He is following his notes and reading aloud. It could be a little in a slow pace. Mr Ku has a good note written during the lesson and he will upload it after the lesson. The content is well organised. It is easy to understand and catch up.
comment_grading: The grading system in MATH1013 is pretty much the same. It seems to have an absolute grading. You could estimate what you may get. However, the course is relatively competitive.. so you have to be extremely careful.
comment_workload: Every one to two weeks there is a online web work. It is fine cause basically it is some basic questions u learnt in the lesson or a little harder. You should be able to handle it if you are following the lesson well.

author: HoraceNg
comment_content:
Lecture 1: Review of numbers, intervals, points and lines, representation of functions
Lecture 2: Review of graph, composite functions, exponential function

Lecture 4: Inverse functions
Lecture 5: The Limit of functions

Lecture 7: Continuity of functions and Intermediate Value Theorem
Lecture 8: Limit at infinity, horizontal asymptotes; tangent line and derivatives

Lecture 11: Chain rule and implicit differentiation
Lecture 12: Derivative of inverse functions etc.

Lecture 14: Differential equations (exponential growth and decay), linear approximation, and Newton's method to find a root
Lecture 15: Maximum and minimum values
Lecture 16: Mean Value Theorem, monotonicity, and concavity
Lecture 17: Local min/max tests (by the 1st derivative and the 2nd derivative), Indeterminate forms and L'Hopital's rule
Lecture 18: Curve Sketch
Lecture 19: Optimization Problems and Antiderivatives
Lecture 20: Antiderivatives and Area/Distance Problems
Lecture 21: Definite integrals
Lecture 22: Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
Lecture 23: Substitution rule

author: Chole
title: 終於讀完呢個course
comment_content:
1. Number and intervals, inequalities and absolutes, functions and graphs, composite functions, exponential functions.

2.Trigonometric functions, inverse functions, logarithmic functions, inverse trigonometric functions.

3.Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws.

4.Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes, derivatives and rates of change.

5.Basic derivatives, product and quotient rules, derivatives of trigonometric functions.

6.Chain rule, implicit differentiation, derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and logarithmic functions.

7.Rates of change problems, related rates.

8.Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values.

9.Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs, L’Hôpital’s rule.

10.Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s Method.

11.Antiderivatives, areas and distances.

12.Definite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

13.Applications of integrals, the substitution rule.

comment_grading:
Grading is bad but clear.
The grade consist 3 part, homework 10%, midterm 35%, final 55%.
All my exams is above mean. And all homework get full marks, which is common due to infinite attempt. But, it's an absolute grading course,  so no need to afraid about the mean. Final grade: B
Usually, >85% A range, >70% B range... Minor arrangement maybe made within this scale.

Recommendation: Never underestimated this course!!! Do the past paper so you can know the real difficulty of the exams. Homework is too easy compared with the exam.

However, with no group project and presentation, all of your grade depends on your exams. The grading is fair.

title: Hoi is my FAVORITE professor this sem!!!
comment_content:
I think if you haven't learned these stuff before, the class is going too fast, and you really need to spend extra time on your own to understand and practice it.

Midterm:
intervals and inequalities, absolute values
functions and graphs, operations on functions, elementary functions, inverse functions
limits of function values and continuity of functions, asymptotes
tangent line equations, derivatives, differentiation rules
derivatives of inverse functions, implicit differentiation, logarithmic differentiation

Midterm topics + Linear Approximation, Rolle's Theorem & Mean Value Theorem, Maximum Minimum and Graph Sketching, L'Hopital's Rule, Optimisation, Newton's Method, Antiderivatives, Riemann Sums, Integrable Functions, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
comment_teaching: He is a new professor from Taiwan. His lectures are very enthusiastic, the class atmosphere is very good, and he likes to interact with students. I was never bored in his class and the time passed quickly. What's more, he is willing to make his lectures better. After class and during class, he actively encourages us to ask questions that we don't understand. For questions that are not answered in time, he will remember these questions and tell you the solution later. Although the average score of our lecture is not very high, his teaching is definitely responsible. There are some key knowledge and examples in his PPT, and he will print out additional exercises for us, but to get a good grade you need to do some additional exercises by yourself. Hoi is definitely a good teacher, I believe the students in his lecture will like him! Hoi's lecture will be a great memory for you.

Final review and/or overview of MATH1014
This is an introductory course in one-variable calculus, the first in the Calculus I and II
sequence, designed for students who have taken HKDSE Mathematics Extended Module
M1/M2 and aim at majoring in mathematics. Major topics include: logic and basic set
theory, functions, injectivity and surjectivity, limits of a function, continuity, differentiability,
derivatives and rules of differentiation, mean value theorem and its consequences.
Important Note: This section L01 is specially designed for first-year students who aim at
majoring in mathematics. A more pure-mathematical approach will be
adopted. At the end of the course, students are expected to be able to
write simple proofs apart from doing mere computations. Lighter
emphasis will be put on real-life applications and mathematical modellings.
Credit points: 3
Prerequisite: Level 3 or above in HKDSE Mathematics Extended Module M1/M2
Exclusions: AL Pure Mathematics or Applied Mathematics, MATH1003/1012/1014/
1018/1020/1023/1024, any MATH course at or above 100-/2000- level
comment_teaching: Extremely good. He is a funny person and he makes everyone understand his words during lectures.
comment_grading: 100% B or above if you treat this course like a dse elective and put as much energy as you have put in studying for dse.
comment_workload:
Written assignments (20%): Assessing ILOs 1, 2, 3 and 4
Homework will be assigned from time to time. You are allowed to have peer discussion
on the solutions, but you need to submit work that is individually written on your own.
The use of ChatGPT or other generative AI tools are not allowed.
You should submit each homework in the form of a clearly written and scanned PDF file
on the Canvas system before the deadline. To accommodate unforeseeable incidents
such as sickness and emergency, the lowest homework score will be dropped from the
calculation of your course total.
 WeBWorK assignments (0%): Assessing ILOs 1 and 3
Although WeBWorK assignments for L02–L11 are not counted towards the final grade for
L01 students, you are encouraged to take a look at it via https://webwork.math.ust.hk/.
 Midterm Test (40%): Assessing ILOs 1, 2, 3 and 4
The mid-term test will be scheduled on Sunday, October 29 from 10:15 to 13:15. It will
tentatively cover all materials from Chapter 0 to Chapter 2.
 Final Exam (40%): Assessing ILOs 1, 2, 3 and 4
The final exam will be scheduled on Friday, December 8 from 12:30 to 15:30. It will
cover everything that has been taught in the course.
The mid-term test and the final exam will normally be closed-book written tests, and
calculators will not be allowed during the tests. The exact exam arrangements may be
modified in the event of unexpected emergencies.

author: elbekjk
title: Quite good if you study hard
comment_content:
You can find others' comments. However, for a person who wants to pursue physics, if theoretical do not bother taking this course and go to the honors version, for experimentalists, you will gain little understanding of the usage in real-life physics.

Tentative Weekly Schedule:
Week 1 Review of numbers, inequalities and absolute values, basic functions and graphs,
composite functions
Week 2 Exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithms, trigonometric functions and
inverse trigonometric functions
Week 3 Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws
Week 4 Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes
Week 5 Derivatives and rates of change, basic derivatives, product and quotient rules
Week 6 Derivatives of trigonometric functions, chain rule, implicit differentiation,
derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and logarithmic functions
Week 7 Rates of change problems, related rates
Week 8
Midterm: Oct 29
Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values
Week 9 Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and the shape of a graph, L'Hopital's rule
Week 10 Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton's method
Week 11 Anti-derivatives, areas and distances, definite integrals
Week 12 The Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, average value of a function
Week 13 Applications of integrals, substitution rule
Study Break
Dec 1-6
OF 33
comment_teaching: The professor was quite good and tried to help every moment, even if you go to his office outside of the office hours.
comment_grading: Grading is, in my opinion, quite appropriate, you will get as much as you work, and it does not include cramming at the last week.
comment_workload: Workload as much as expected.

author: IncomingCGA4.0
title: M2 ppl excel, M1 ppl (true for most) will die
comment_content: The first few lectures involve simple core dse math, like index law and graph transformation. But then it steps into basic differentiation and basic integration. Theorems like Newton's Method, Intermediate Value Theorem, Mean Value Theorem and L'Hospital Rule will be included.
comment_teaching:
Professor Wang has a really really strong mainland accent, which is quite intrusive to understanding the idea she's trying to deliver. That's why I attended only the first few lectures and skipped all others. Her tone and voice also makes you fall asleep easily. However, her notes are all hand-written, which shows she's quite passionate at teaching, but not sure if she'll actively answer students' questions. Same for its tutorials.
No attendance, no quiz, no projects, recordings provided and notes with annotations uploaded after lectures.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading.
WebWork (10%): 900/900
Midterm (35%): 79/100
Final (55%): Not yet released when I'm writing this review
Heard that you have to get like 70% for B range, and 80% for A range.
comment_workload: Depends on what grade you wanna get and how good is your foundation. Be noted that this is not a very simple course and you need to devote time into practicing with pastpapers and attempting WebWork questions, and you may still receive an unsatisfactory grade.

55% Final
comment_workload: Unlimited attempts for webwork so you can brute force through some of the questions. The questions are also relatively easy and you only need to spend 1.5hr max per week.

author: 命運無常
title: From the perspective of a student getting LV.4 in DSE M2 (Good luck if you studied M1)
comment_content:
Overall: B (3.12)

Course outline (40%): B+
Quality of course material (60%): B

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

📙 Introductory course to calculus. Topics are not too hard, but it takes you some time to fully understand and apply them.

📚 New // old but more in-depth topics compared to 2023 DSE M2:
1. Inverse functions, vertical line test and horizontal line test
2. One-side limit, continuity and differentiability
3. Squeeze theorem and Intermediate theorem
4. Normal line
5. Derivatives of cot(x), sec(x)and csc(x) and inverse trigonometric functions
6. Exponential growth and decay
7. Mean value theorem and L'Hopital's rule
8. Antiderivatives of some (inverse) trigonometric and other functions
9. Riemann sum

⭐ Feel free to use my learning resources for revision.

https://drive.google.com/drive/u/1/folders/18dV2WNhDV4_gidQVaWqJiBmpMAhUVUGp
comment_teaching:
Overall: B- (2.87)

Professor Wang (90%): B
TA (Daniel Cheng) (10%): C-

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

1. Professor Wang

🇨🇳 Mainland professor

✅ Her hand-written notes are detailed with many examples.

✅ She uses Piazza to answer students' questions. I think it is useful for students to solve their questions.

⚠️ Her teaching style is quite boring. Not many students attend her lectures after 4-5 lessons.

⚠️ Also, the examples in lecture notes are much more simple than questions in exams. You should find extra questions with higher difficulties by yourself.

2. TA

🇭🇰 Local TA

⚠️ His teaching is very boring. His voice and English are even much weaker than the professor's, making students easily fall asleep. I only attended the first lesson and skipped all his tutorials after knowing that no need to take attendance.

✅ You can download his tutorial notes 2-3 days after each tutorial. I think his tutorial notes are fine for revision (but his questions are easier than exams' questions)
comment_grading:
Overall: C+ (2.59)

Appropriateness of weightings (25%): A-
Appropriateness of difficulties of assignments (10%): B+
Appropriateness of difficulty of exams (35%): C
Transparency (10%): B+
Ease of aiming A-range (15%): C

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

Grading is not good.

⚠️ In the exam, One MCQ weights 5 points and there are only 3-5 LQ, in which each of them usually weights around 7-10 points (sometimes over 10 points).
⚠️ No calculator is allowed in exams, so only little mistakes are allowed to get A range.

⚠️ Most people fall into C range, only around 1/4 of students can get B range or above, so it is quite difficult.

📝 General Grading system:
F < 40% < D < 50% < C < 70% < B < 85% < A

10% WeBworK
35% Mid-term
55% Final exam

⚠️ The course adapt absolute grading system. Attached document is the actual grading system for this semester.

✏️ Results:

1. Mid-term exam
My score: 73/100
Mean: 61.18
S.D.: 17.65

2. Final exam
My score: 77/100
Mean: 66.76 (for my session only)

3. Homework
Get 100% for all 9 assignments

💯 Total score: 77.9/100
💯 Grade: B

FYI LV.4 in both DSE 2023 Mathematics (Compulsory part) and M2
comment_workload:
Overall: C- (1.94)

Assignments (15%): B+
Exams (85%): C-

---------------------------------------------------------------------------

✅ 1 assignment per 1-1.5 weeks. You have around 2 weeks to finish each assignmnet, which contains around 10-15 questions.

✅ You have to use WeBworK to answer questions. Unlimited attempts are allowed. The questioms are very simple compared to questions in exams

⭐ To get at least B range, quite a lot of practices are needed, but two to three careless mistakes can let you fall below A range, so check your answers at least twice before moving onto next question.

mean value theorem, one-to-one function,
sandwich (squeeze) theorem, linear approximation,
Newton's method, critical point, Rolle's theorem,

comment_teaching:
Lam Tsz Kin has a way better english accent than the other prof tbh, and he is willing to answer student's questions, but the teaching is equally boring. The notes of him are unorganized. I only attended the first week.
If you want to catch up with the progress but are not willing to attend lesson, could attend tutorials every week. The tutorials basically cover the content of the lectures. So, don't worry, be happy.
comment_grading:
10% WebWork 35% Mid-term 55% Final.

Very good grading,  Absolute grading. Minor adjustment when the mean is not satifactory. If you scored 85% or above, secure A range.
However, I finally got a B because I messed up the exam with 60 marks.
WEBWORK 10% need secure.
comment_workload:
Not really a heavy workload, given the fact that I did not attend any lessons. Webwork is easy calculus and unlimited attempts.
One mid-term and one final.

author: chuhao
title: not for m1 student
comment_content:
Calculus. Half of it is DSE-M2 and half of it is new. If M1, good luck to you.

The following is a partial list of the non-M2 materials in this course:

Existence, Differentiability, and Theorem of Intermediate Values, 'One-to-One' function, Mean Value Theorem using absolute functions to solve difficulties, Theorem of Sandwich (Squeeze), approximate linearly Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Critical Point, Rolle's Theorem, L'Hopital Rule, Reimann Sum, and a few other concepts (a few more than DSE M2).
comment_teaching: Dr. Lam is happy to answer inquiries from students and speaks fluent English. However, his writing is less organized on the whiteboard, making it difficult to follow. The pace of instruction is sluggish, and he spent an excessive amount of time explaining the fundamental concepts that should have been covered in M1, M2, and even the DSE Maths core, particularly functions.
comment_workload: There is often one WebWork (online homework) every week, with 10 to 18 questions in each. With the exception of the first, each task should take you between two and four hours to complete. Try your hardest to gain all the points possible on the questions since you can try them as many times as you like.

author: liquid
comment_content: The course is about the fundamental theorems of calculus. It mainly introduces the knowledge of limit and differential. If you studied M2 at DSE or have a foundation in calculus, the course should be easy for you.
comment_teaching: Teaching is good, and class notes are pre-uploaded to canvas.The notes are handwritten in advance for easy comprehension.If you didn't go to class, a recording of each session will also be uploaded to canvas for you to learn from.
comment_grading:
Absolute Grading
A+ 95% or above
A Range 85% or above
B Range 65% or above
C Range 50% or above
Passing Grade 40% or above
During the semester, the final grade requirements are more relaxed than this table, and even if you don't reach a certain percentage, the professor will give you a better letter grade depending on your final grade.
comment_workload:
Attendance is not calculated for this course.
The homework is 20 questions per week and can be repeated an unlimited number of times.
If you have a foundation in calculus, the workload may seem very small to you. If not, then the midterms and final may be a little difficult for you.

author: yanjai
title: Chill
comment_content:
1. Number and intervals, inequalities and absolutes, functions and graphs, composite functions, exponential functions.

2.Trigonometric functions, inverse functions, logarithmic functions, inverse trigonometric functions.

3.Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws.

4.Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes, derivatives and rates of change.

5.Basic derivatives, product and quotient rules, derivatives of trigonometric functions.

6.Chain rule, implicit differentiation, derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and logarithmic functions.

7.Rates of change problems, related rates.

8.Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values.

9.Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs, L’Hôpital’s rule.

10.Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s Method.

11.Antiderivatives, areas and distances.

12.Definite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

13.Applications of integrals, the substitution rule.
comment_teaching: basically repeats slides so study yourself. This a good opportunity to equip yourself with the ability to self-learning.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading, F < 40% < D < 50% < C's < 70% < B's < 85% < A's

It's like a more competitive version of DSE, you train more you score more. So dun be surprised to get a low mark. Derivations of theorems are more or less useless if you are not a L1 student so just skip right into drilling lol.
comment_workload: It depends. If you know the way to drill those papers and also have a not-bad math sense (like at least a 5 in m2 lol), and also start revision only like a week ahead of the exam, you are secured to get a B something. A range surely requires a constant effort in knowing the concepts thoroughly and drilling the papers regularly.

author: KrazyKaren
title: Math madness
comment_content:
1. Functions
2. Limits
3. Derivatives and their applications
4. Integration
comment_teaching:
Professor Ku is passionate about the subject matter, which shines through in his teaching style. However, his monotone delivery can make the class feel less dynamic. Also, he looks at the ceiling while teaching which is hilarious.
Nonetheless, I would not recommend attending the lectures, as the material covered is fairly easy. Instead, I suggest attending Phyllis Liang's tutorial sessions. Her tutorial notes are exceptional, and by reviewing them before attempting past papers, you'll find significant assistance in your preparation.
comment_grading:
The difficulty ranking for the course components is as follows:

1. Lectures: Considered the least challenging aspect of the course.
2. Homework: Slightly more difficult than lectures, but quite useless.
3. Tutorials: Some tutorial questions are taken from homework and past year papers, making them moderately challenging.
4. Past papers: More challenging than tutorials as they provide a comprehensive review of the course material.
5. Actual Exam: The most difficult component, because they know the course is absolute grading and they don't want you to get a grade higher than B range.

Since the grading is absolute, your performance in the course will solely depend on your understanding and preparation.

The following is the rough guideline: A+/A/A- > 85.5 > B+/B/B- >70.5 > C+/C/C- > 50.5 > D > 40
comment_workload: The workload for this course is relatively light, with only a few assignments every week. These assignments can be attempted unlimited times, so try to get full mark for this.

author: ccnncmjt

comment_content: The course material is comparable to that of HKDSE Mathematics Module 2, but with addition of new topics such as Riemann sums
comment_teaching: I would not recommend selecting this professor due to his heavy accent, which can make the lessons difficult to follow and boring. However, the tutorials are beneficial since they provide worksheets with practice questions that closely resemble those on the actual exam. Additionally, the tutor leading the tutorials injects humour into the sessions. Attendance is not taken for either the lectures or tutorials.
comment_grading: The grading system is fair and score above 85 can get A- or above. The average score for the midterm exam is approximately 75, while the average for the final exam is around 55. Furthermore, the cutoff for the grade is slightly lower by one or two marks in this semester.
comment_workload: The workload for this course is relatively light, with only 9 homework assignment to complete on WebWork. To achieve a good grade, it is recommended to simply complete the past paper before the exam.

author: MarDing
title: MATH 1013 Course Review
comment_content:
Ch.1
Review of functions
Representing functions
Inverse, exponential and logarithmic functions
Trigonometric functions and their inverses

Ch.2
The idea of limits
Definitions of limits
Techniques for computing limits
Infinite limits
Limits at infinity
Continuity

Ch.3
Introducing the derivatives
Rules of differentiation
The product and quotient rules
Derivatives of trigonometric functions
Derivatives as rates of change
The chain rule
Implicit differentiation
Derivatives of logarithmic and exponential functions
Derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions
Related rates

Ch.4
Maxima and minima
What derivatives tell us
Graphing functions
Optimization problems
Linear approximation and differentials
Mean value theorem
L'Hopital's rule
Newton's method
Antiderivatives

Ch.5
Approximating areas under curves
Definite integrals
Fundamental theorem of calculus
Working with integrals
Substitution rule


This is an introductory course in one-variable calculus, the first in the Calculus I and II sequence, designed for students that have taken HKDSE Mathematics Extended Module M1/M2. Topics include functions and their limits, continuity, derivatives and rules of differentiation, applications of derivatives, and basic integral calculus. Exclusion(s): AL Pure Mathematics; AL Applied Mathematics; MATH 1003, MATH 1012, MATH 1014, MATH 1018, MATH 1020, MATH 1023, MATH 1024 Prerequisite(s): Level 3 or above in HKDSE Mathematics Extended Module M1/M2
comment_teaching:
Teaching according to the textbook 'Calculus for Scientists and Engineers (Early Transcendentals) by Briggs, Cochran and Gillett' (Chapter 1 to 5).

TK Lam will do the proving part of the theorem in the lecture lessons. However the textbook has already provides the detail proving with some examples.
TK Lam taught well about the theoretical part but seldom provide practice questions and applications.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading system.
The following is the rough guideline:
A+/A/A- > 85.5 > B+/B/B- >70.5 > C+/C/C- > 50.5 > D > 40

No marks for attendance.

My result:
Mid-term: 90/100 (35%)
final exam: 79/100 (55%)
Homework: 10/10 (10%)
Overall: 84.95/100
Letter grade: B+
It is so sad that I nearly can get a A- in this course due to the absolute grading system even I got higher than the mean about 20 marks.
comment_workload: The workload is very light that it only requires to do assignments on the WebWork system, which is around 10 assignments totally. The assignments can help you to review the concepts and its applications in various kind of questions. You can submit the answer for several times which will not deduct your marks.

<40 Fail

Easy to handle and get B range or above for M2 people

For non-M2 people, it may be a bit hard to compete with M2 guys, yet if you do all the assignments it is achievable to get B range or higher

comment_grading:
有2種計法

HW：20%

Final：40%
總共：100%

方法2:
HW：20%
Midterm：5%
Final：70%

個人（、同學、阿sir）心得就係：

1.先睇清楚題目given咩比你，留意我最後想要咩結果
2.千萬5好由尾(result)做到頭(given)（prove既題目），要根據題目given既野做到結果
3.5識就試下將睇得明既野畫圖出來先

（4） .千萬5好死記公式，要記得還要記得，但你記得對FInal/Midterm只係一DD作用，你需要既係對concept既理解（因為份卷一半多D題目都係同你之前做既題目/練習5同）

author: Cvsvdvdv
comment_content:
Calculus. Half DSE-M2 stuff, half new things. Good luck if you are M1.
Here's a list(not complete) of non-M2 stuffs in this course:
Continuity, Differentiability, Intermediate Value Theorem, Mean Value Theorem, 'one-to-one' function, Solving problems with absolute functions, Sandwich(Squeeze) Theorem, Linear Approximation, Newton's Method, Critical Point, Rolle's Theorem, L'Hopital Rule, Reimann Sum, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus(A little more things than DSE M2)
comment_teaching:
Instructors don't matter, the number of monstrous mainland students in your lecture does.

I highly recommend that you:
1. Read James Stweart Calculus 7ed (this course covers part of the book).
2. Finish your homework ASAP, get extra problem sets if you're not confident in a specific chapter.
3. Get yourself prepared for the test by doing pastpaper.
4. MOST IMPORTANT: Know what you're doing. Know how and WHY you're carrying out the calculation in a particular way instead of another.
comment_grading:

author: worstUSTstudent
comment_content:
Number and intervals, inequalities and absolutes, functions and graphs, composite functions, exponential functions.
Trigonometric functions, inverse functions, logarithmic functions, inverse trigonometric functions.
Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws.
Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes, derivatives and rates of change.
Basic derivatives, product and quotient rules, derivatives of trigonometric functions.
Chain rule, implicit differentiation, derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and logarithmic functions.
Rates of change problems, related rates.
Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values.
Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs, L’Hôpital’s rule.
Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s Method.
Antiderivatives, areas and distances.
Definite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Applications of integrals, the substitution rule. (sorry)

favourable to people with good grade in DSE M2
comment_teaching:
I think skipping all lectures is fine as long as you understand the concepts of the course and do past paper.

Both lectures and tutorials do not require attendance.
comment_grading:
10% Homework
35% Midterm
55% Final

I think is quite decent although I got a super bad grade. It's either because I am dumb or didn't revise much or both. I think it is both.
comment_workload: Webwork is super easy and you must get 100% of all homework because you have unlimited tries. Webwork also doesn't reflect the difficulty of midterms and finals at all. Please do past papers.

author: UST sucks
comment_content:
In choronological order:
1. The content is rather simple in the first few weeks, basically about derivatives. Do pay attention to the definition of derivatives (the one that uses limit to express derivatives), usually harder questions in midterm exam come from this knowledge point.

2. Knowledge points whichever relevant to limits are EXTREMELY important!

3. To me, things get harder quickly when it comes to integration. Time seems going really fast in the last few weeks. For those who have no foundation like me, I STRONGLY suggest previewing this part in the first few weeks, as you may have more spare time at that time to practice and digest.
comment_teaching:
Prof. Wang is nice! Her notes are neat and clear, her teaching pace is just the way I like (though may be a bit slow for students who are good at maths), and she is very prompt and patient when answering questions. What's more, she do listen to students' needs, as she gave us quite some materials for revision before final in respond to some students' email. As a professor teaching maths, she've already done everything.
She has the ability to explain concepts clear, so no need to search for other online courses.
comment_grading:
Quite fair.
10% Homework
35% Midterm
55% Final
Questions in homework allow you to submit answers in unlimited times, so it is worthwhlie bringing more patience to finish them perfectly, and raise questions whenever you encounter problem.
Midterm is easy, do avoid making stupid mistakes.
Final is hard, so enough practice is necessary
My grades:
full in homework
Midterm: 85/100 (mean:78.07)
Final: 74/100 (mean:55.68)
Total: 80.45 B+ (mean:67.44)
comment_workload:
Quite friendly even to year1 students. Nothing more than Webwork assignments.
However, my suggestion is, it is not realistic to get A range if you solely rely on exercise in homework and tutorial. Extra exercise is a must 'cuz competition is fierce.

author: aaron1014
title: The average student's review
comment_content:
Although the contents of this course were mostly covered in high school, it was designed to be delivered in a more in depth manner (at least compared to my high school calculus). Definitely gave me a better understanding of how calculus works.

Main topics covered:
    - Functions (including composite, exponential, inverse, log, trigonometric)
    - Limits (heavy emphasis on this for the midterms)
    - Differentiation (derivatives, implicit differentiation)
    - Related rates
    - Linear approximation
    - Mean value theorem
    - L'hopital rule
    - Curve sketching (this is a pain in the ass)
    - Optimization
    - Newton's method
    - Integration (Riemann sum)
    - Fundamental theorem of calculus
    - Substitution rule
comment_teaching:
Class lecturer: Bian Fengmiao

The teacher had a heavy accent when teaching, but she gave amazing digitally hand written lecture notes (both effective and visually appealing). Examples in class cover a large variations of questions to help you grasp the concepts. She teaches by solving pre written questions by hand similar to how math YouTubers do it. The working she does in class are pretty much the content of the lecture notes she provides after the class.

Tutor: LAU Hing Sang

Amazing tutor. Provides you a tutorial sheet with a ton of questions to practice on and has a website for the answers. Discusses a wide variety of questions in the actual tutorial so your time is definitely not wasted. To be honest, there's more value attending this tutor than the actual lectures. Might have graded the teaching lower if not for this tutor.
comment_grading:
Midterm: 75/100 (Mean: 76.1)
Final: 66/100 (Mean: 59.48)
Total: 72.55/100 (Mean: 68.86)
Letter grade: B

For midterms and finals, I did around 3-4 past papers. Had some careless answers in the exams. Past papers are similar to each other. Overall I think the grading was pretty fair, although I think quite a few would disagree
comment_workload: The only workload outside of class was the web work homework.It has unlimited attempts. I think it would be nice to find someone who also takes the course to discuss the homework. Overall, I think the workload was light especially if you compare it to other subjects.

Prof Lam uses pen and paper to demonstrate examples in class.

started self-studying after mid-term. performed much better in finals than in mid-term :P
comment_grading:
Absolute Grading.

Grade scale:   F < 40% < D < 50% < C's < 70% < B's < 85% < A's

Grading system:
- WeBWorK Online Homework (10%)
- Midterm Exam (35%)
- Final Exam (55%)

Mean for mid-term is around 7x%
Mean for finals is around 5x%
comment_workload:

WebWork assignment around once per 2 weeks' time.
Each assignment has around 10 questions and you can finish it with unlimited trials.

As a DSE M1 student, I find the concepts easy to understand but the questions are way more complicated than expected.

author: Ayushd
comment_content:
- No attendance taken
- The syllabus and the exam covers the fundamentals of Calculus (mainly applicative Calculus)
- The designated textbook is extremely useful. Numerous practise questions are given.
comment_teaching: The lecture was not very interesting to me as the professor used PPT to teach. The content of PPT has been directly taken from the textbook.
comment_grading: Scoring a desent grade is easy if you avoid silly mistakes such as calculation error. The invigilator will not tolerate silly errors.
comment_workload: If you are familiar with the fundamentals of Calculus, then the workload will be pretty less. Weekly assignment will be easy to do if the core concepts are clear. Solving questions from the textbook is beneficial.

author: Warp Drive
comment_content: The course title is Calculus for Math Majors so undoubtedly it focuses a lot more on the rigorous definition of limit and there are a number of proof rather than calculation in both of your homework and exams. The content, homework and exams are quite different from other lectures of MATH1013. From my own point of view, it is closer to MATH 1023 rather than other 1013. This course covers contents from limit to derivative. Integration will be taught in 1014L1.
comment_teaching:
Prof Frederick Fong is definitely an outstanding teacher. He appreciates the beauty of rigorism of mathematics and emphasizes that in his class from time to time. Although I attended only a few lectures, I was deeply impressed by how clearly the professor illustrates things.
As for the tutorial, I had never attended that but I downloaded every tutorial handouts. They are quite helpful when preparing for the exams.
comment_grading:
There are two grading schemes.
Scheme A: homework 20%; midterm 40%; final 40% (total 100%)
Scheme B: homework 20%; midterm 5%; final 70% (total 95%)
Obviously you can find that there is a chance to make up if your midterm score is not that satisfactory---but at the expense of losing 5% in your total score anyway. It turned out that except for those who get 0 marks in their midterms, almost no one actually benefits from the Scheme B so midterm is crucial.
Statistics:
midterm: LQ 49; mean 63.78; UQ 81.5; high 100 (total 100)
final: LQ 41; mean 53.13; UQ 68; high 99.5 (total 100)
I got 97.48 in homework, 90 in midterm and 80.5 in final, my letter grade is A- (very close to A)
A range is only 15% with half A-, which, from my own view, is a little harsh considering the difficulty of the exams. But don't worry, the B and C ranges are wide enough.
More detailed statistics is attached.
comment_workload: The content of the lecture is quite easy. Homework is not on webwork. You need to print it and handwrite. The questions on lecture and tutorial notes along with sample midterm/final are main resources for you to prepare for the exam since there is no past paper. It is a bit annoying since the questions on lecture notes are not with answers so sometimes you need to think for a long time to solve some proof questions. Luckily, the questions in exams are not very difficult but you need to BE CAREFUL ABOUT THE DETAILS in your solution. Sadly, I always skipped some steps or didn't follow the standard step of mathematical induction in exams. So although I got the correct answer or my method of proving is correct, I lost 5~8 points in both exams and it cannot be argued back.
