Week 7 Rates problems
Week 8 Linear approximations, Newton's method, L'Hopital's rule
Week 9 Extreme values of functions,  Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs
Week 10 Curve sketcking, antiderivatives
Week 11 Areas and definite integrals
Week 12 Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, applications of integrals
Week 13   Substitution rule
comment_teaching: he will not use notes, but quite good teaching by demonstrate examples, not hard for dse m2ers
comment_grading:
55%final
35%mid term
10%web work(unlimited attempt)
no attendance
comment_workload: 1 webwork per week

author: clt
title: M2 2
comment_content:
Basically a review of the M2 syllabus but with a bit more stuff extended, like differentiability, IVT, MVT, the latter two are only introduced close to the middle of the course. You can reference other people's reviews for the syllabus. You should be able to handle the concepts if you are versed in M2, though from the other reviews it won't give you a free ticket to a good grade.

1013 leans more on the application of calculus, and less on the rigorous proving side of things. I observed 1023 in this semester, and the difference between them is the application vs proving focus. If you're interested in things akin to https://www.youtube.com/playlist?list=PLjCP7F6SlypXhD8vlv_wP-pZbn_-gZ3sd you might prefer 1023, but for me I just needed to fill the prerequisite with either 1013/1023.
comment_teaching:
No attendance for tutorials nor lectures. Officially you have to pick a tutorial with matching tutorial numbers with the lectures, but obviously they don't enforce that.

He basically just reads off the notes he gives in canvas. I don't think I got anything out of the lectures, save for the classwork exercises and getting to ask him questions about the notes. Though he is still approachable, after-class or email.
comment_grading:
Will update after finals, but I got 97% on the midterms with 1 LQ mistake.

A majority of the marks were on MC questions. No calculators are allowed, but there weren't any calculations that warranted a calculator with the large amount of time given to us anyways.
comment_workload: Completed in WebWork, 1-2 weeks per HW(? don't remember precisely). There is adequate time to read ahead and finish them or wait until the lecturer gets to the topic. Around 10 Qs per HW, math support center is always there if you needed help.

author: Harr
title: Math 1013 L1
comment_content:
similar to 1013 other sections but focus more  on logic and writing proof
the cotent is based on the notes provided by the professor
and the best supplement materials is HKALE past paper
comment_teaching:
excellent teaching
Prof. Fong always give out some joke during the lessons to catch you attention
every concept is explained very detail
the best professor in math department (professor A0)
comment_grading:
absolute grading
only those who have 94 or above can get A+
the mean of midterm is ~66
comment_workload:
not heavy at all
compare to 1023, 1013 workload is close to 0

author: Lgu
comment_content: Basic calculus things and quite similar to m2, if you are an m1 student, better go study m2 yourselves first and learn some calculus techniques like implicit differentiation, log differentiation, trigonometry. I’m also a m1 student but I think these things are not difficult to understand.But if you don’t learn these things yourself than you will have totally no idea for what’s going on in the lectures, then start revising one week before the exam and deep fry in the midterm and say: oh I’m a m1 student, it is unfair!
comment_teaching: The teaching is quite mundane and not too many students are listening.80% of things in the lectures are about proof and logics, and 20%useful things are interspersed in these things so self study is important.
comment_grading: The homework can be done for unlimited times,so everyone get a full mark.The midterm and final exams is the key of grading so study hard before exams.
comment_workload: The work load is not high.Homework every week and it will take you maybe one hour to complete, and you can try infinite times so you don’t need to think too carefully before summiting.

author: fifio
comment_content:
1. Number and intervals, inequalities and absolutes, functions and graphs, composite functions, exponential functions.

2.Trigonometric functions, inverse functions, logarithmic functions, inverse trigonometric functions.

3.Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws.

4.Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes, derivatives and rates of change.

5.Basic derivatives, product and quotient rules, derivatives of trigonometric functions.

6.Chain rule, implicit differentiation, derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and logarithmic functions.

7.Rates of change problems, related rates.

8.Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values.

9.Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs, L’Hôpital’s rule.

10.Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s Method.

11.Antiderivatives, areas and distances.

12.Definite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

13.Applications of integrals, the substitution rule.
comment_teaching: Teaching is okay but you have to absolutely study more outside of class, you will not understand anything on midterms just relying on lectures.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading, F < 40% < D < 50% < C's < 70% < B's < 85% < A's
For our session, mean is 66.16/100
So, our mean is C grade!
comment_workload: Homework every week but you get to redo until you get 100. Does not really gives a view on the midterm, midterm is way harder! PLEASE DO YOUR OWN REVIEW AFTER CLASS

comment_grading:
The payoff is proportional to the reward.

comment_grading: If you are hard-working, you can get a decent or above-average grade. The grading depends partly on assignments, and partly on the mid-term and final exams.

author: LL.
title: Not vert good experience
comment_content:
Even if you study M2, you need to study hard as well for this course. Good luck for those M1 students :)

Here's a list(not complete/update) of  stuffs in this course:
Continuity, Differentiability, Intermediate Value Theorem, Mean Value Theorem, 'one-to-one' function, Solving problems with absolute functions, Sandwich(Squeeze) Theorem, Linear Approximation, Newton's Method, Critical Point, Rolle's Theorem, L'Hopital Rule, Reimann Sum, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (credit from previous review, I think it's a good reference)
comment_teaching:
If you want to concentrate in the class and outperform, please find an instructor who you can listen what he said.

Make sure to attend tutorial classes as well, a good TA really helps a lot, with exercises and clearing concepts!!!

Generally speaking, I do think attending tutorial is more important than attending lecture. If you have a good TA with good materials and do not skip concepts.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading system, which makes this course VERY HARD to get a good grade:
85-100%-->A range
70-85%-->B range
50-70%-->C range
40-50%-->D
0-40%-->Fail

Think before you take the course :)
comment_workload:
Homework:
Basically 1 WebWork(Online homework) per week, each consists of 10-18 questions.

No attendance required for any lecture and tutorials as usual.

Exam: Do your best in the relatively simple mid-term to secure a better grade earlier. All contents will appear in final exam, so really a lot to review before the exam. The questions could be very hard but not undoable.

Grade is B-

Even though I did all homework, I thought I will get at least B.
I would say grading is bad, so do not mess up your final.
comment_workload:
8 Homework (No written homework, but web-work).

Some of the work require a very longtime to deal with, so make sure you start doing it at least 3 days before the deadline. A similar solution is available in internet.

author: Billy Ngai
comment_content:
Half DSE-M2 stuff, half new things..
Here's a list(not complete) of non-M2 stuffs in this course:
Continuity, Differentiability, Intermediate Value Theorem, Mean Value Theorem, 'one-to-one' function, Solving problems with absolute functions, Sandwich(Squeeze) Theorem, Linear Approximation, Newton's Method, Critical Point, Rolle's Theorem, L'Hopital Rule, Reimann Sum, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus(A little more things than DSE M2)
comment_teaching: Just repeating the notes.When I first go to tutorial,there was only 2 people in the classroom.After that, I never attend tutorial again
comment_grading: Above mean0.5 sd,get a B
comment_workload: About 10 assignment,time-consuming but not hard

author: Sune
comment_content:
So first, from DSE M2, limits, differentiation, very minimal integration, and the trigonometry things.

Then Linear Approximation, MinMax values of a function, Mean Value Theorem, Graphing, L'Hopital's Rule, Sketch curves, Newton's Method, Antiderivatives, Areas and Disatances, Definite Integrals, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, Substitution Rule, Applications of Integrals.

I may have skipped some stuff but the above are the more important stuff.

Do not underestimate the content, they seem semi-easy in the first month, but then the difficulty ramps up like walking up a giraffe's back.
comment_teaching:
For this lecturer, you wanna know her teaching quality?
Imagine a normal teaching, then throw that all out the window.
Her accent is questionable, You would really have to focus to comprehend what she is saying. However, it's not really that difficult because you have the PowerPoint slides as subtitles.
To be fair, she would happily answer any of your questions and her 1-on-1 helping is infinitely better than her lecturing.
comment_grading:
HW 10%
Midterm 35%
Final 55%

Our midterm this year was 10 MCs, that's it, 10 multiple choice questions. Get one wrong you lose 10%, i.e. 3.5 marks in the final grade.
Finals was 18 MCs(not counting the one that asks you what version your paper is) and 3 LQs, it was a bit more bearable, with MC bearing 54 marks and LQ 46.

OH YES DON'T FORGET ONE THING: NO CALCULATORS IN MIDTERM & FINALS.

I screwed up (failed, got 30%) my midterm, did better in finals and full marks in HW and got a C+ at the end.

Do not underestimate the difficulty of the exam, try to find past papers elsewhere and definitely do the sample paper, but remember the actual is wayyyy harder than the sample paper.
comment_workload: Not that much, we had HW on classviva, and they allow infinite tries with no penalty, so you can try and try and try. I do have to warn that the HW gets more tough near the end of the semester though, so you should always start early because there's almost no way you're deadline fighting the assignments if you wanna get full mark on HW (which I recommend anyone to do so).

author: Yippppp
comment_content: It covers quite a lot of dse m2 content, but learning m2 in high-school definitely is not enough to secure good grade in the course.
comment_teaching:
The professor will use his own pad to illustrate examples which are quite detailed.
He will also stress on the proof and definition of different theorem in order to let students have a clearer understanding.
Moreover, he is willing to stop whenever someone is not getting the points.
He is passionate to solve any questions raised by students at any time of the lesson.
comment_grading: The grading of this course is absolute grading, which means even if u higher than the mean 1x marks, you can only get a B, whereas other maths courses are mainly grade by curve.
comment_workload: It is okay workload, some online exercise which allow infinite attempts. So you can repeat it until you get 100.

author: Kaisa

Since the textbook is written by him, most examples given in lectures are same as textbook. However, you should not miss lectures since some important explanation is helpful for your progress and problem solving ability.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading.
10% by online homework(Unlimited attempt so you can correct until get full marks)
35% by midterm exam
55% by final exam
No attendance
Midterm is disaster since there is only 10 MC and you lose ten marks per mistake...if you make more than one mistakes in midterm like me, you can never expect a A+ since the total score cannot reach 93.5(A+line)

final:96(mean 73.14)
total grade:90.8(mean 71.35)
Final grade:A
comment_workload:
Fair workload.
Online homework get increasingly difficult, and do not worry too much if you cannot finish homework 7 or 8 easily. Most students get great pain here and final exam is not as difficult as them.
I really regretted missing lectures before midterm. Just go to lectures(WeiPing's lecture is great), do homework carefully and review before exam. Do enough past papers and you will find it easy.

author: Mr.a
comment_content: All about calculus. M2 background is preferred. ~30% of the content is learnt in M2. There will be much more theorems and extensions about calculus. Basically the whole M2 syllabus is included, except for the volume calculation.
comment_teaching:
She has a strong mainland accent and inaccurate pronunciation. She just basically reads the slides word by word every lesson. Paying attention during the lectures is not helping. I self-study the whole course just by doing ex and reading slides.

The tutorials are better than the lectures. The TA makes her own notes and explains the question in it explicitly. She will post the answers after every lesson. It's not hard to follow the pace of the tutorials as well.

PS attendance not counted in tutorials
comment_grading:
Homework: 10%
Midterm: 35%
Final: 55%

Midterm has 10 mc questions only. Final has 19 mc questions and 3 long questions. No calculator is allowed in both exams.

Grading is good to me. I got full in hw, 90% in midterm and 97% in final, overall 94.85%, got an A+. Didn't expect that good.

For your reference, I got level 5 in M2 and 5* in compulsory part.
comment_workload: Depends on how hard you work. If you want to get a good grade, you should do some questions in the question bank in classviva. Homework is not extremely hard. You can have unlimited tries, so 100% in hw shouldn't be hard.

author: Wai Ching
title: DSE M2 did not help much for this course. Pastpaper is beneficial. How smart you are greatly affect your performance
comment_content: Lots of review on some DSE M2 as the course was slanted to M2 rather than M1. Although many content overlapped with M2, the questions in mid-term and final exam were not straight forward. Some even indirectly asked for the fundamentals of the content like testing the understandings of derivatives by providing graphs that seemed to be irrelevant with the course materials. It would help if you complete past papers or tutorials questions as they seemed alike. The questions were in identical format.
comment_teaching:
Lecturer Jin was so patient in explaining the details which I appreciate but this might be a drawback. He allowed students to interrupt if students could not hear or understand the contents immediately. Some students repeatedly asked some silly questions even if Jin had already answered. I could not stand it as mid-term and exam did not cover those. Therefore I did not attend any lectures after mid-term.

TA Phyllis taught the questions in detail, basically step-by-step. Attend the tutorials if you like to listen the details. But I was reluctant to attend them except the first month as answers were provided a week later. Therefore I completed it by myself and checked the answers when provided.

All lectures/tutorials did not count attendance so I would not recommend attending them or not.
comment_grading:
Classviva Homework:10%
Mid-term:35%(10 MCs)
FInal:55%(18MCs & 3LQs)

Please refer to the attachment for the statistics and my grades.

For mid-term, although it only consisted of 10MCs, they required understanding the basics rather than memorising the question types.

For final exam, MCs were similar to those in mid-term, LQs were straight forward but if you failed to study some specific part, you could not complete it(I did not study/understood Riemann Sum and Newton method thus losing quite a lot of marks)

Be careful with the questions as they were kinda tricky due to subtle changes with the wordings.
comment_workload:
For homework it was not heavy as unlimited attempts allowed and answers may be available online by copying the whole question to search on Internet with detailed explanations.

For revision even if you were familiar with the question types and the content, it may not be easy since variations plus cross topics were possible in tests and final. It greatly depends on your flexibility between concepts. Even for MCs it took a lot of time for some questions, especially no calculators allowed for assessments.

Topics include functions and their limits, continuity, derivatives and rules of differentiation, applications of derivatives, and basic integral calculus.
comment_teaching:

But I did attended the tutorial session, tutorial is somewhat useful as it provides an opportunity to you to do some maths and know what could be asked in the exam.

55% final exam
comment_workload: 8 classviva assignments with unlimited attempts, so you can easily score full marks. No other homeworks or projects so the workload is relatively small.

comment_grading: The grading for the workload is great since you can repeat infinitely for your homework. However, for mid-term and final...You should put a lot of effort to receive the grades that you want
comment_workload: Although the workload is quite heavy, I gave B since you can try as many time as you want. The grading for the workload is based on your highest grade, not the average of your attempts.

comment_teaching:
I attend the pure online mode of this course despite I am in Hong Kong in this semester.

Professor Jin's teaching is acceptable. He is still a nice guy.

He uses his iPad Pro to copy the content from the textbook and illustrate the example to us.

Sometimes his accent is hard to understand and I have to ask him to repeat before I can understand.

He can answer my questions clearly each time I ask him through email or face-to-face.

However, the teaching of the TA is barely acceptable.

The TA is called SHI, Zhongyi. He is really boring and I can hardly understand what he said. The interaction level is really low that we are just watching how he uses his iPad to demonstrate the application of the mathematics skills we learn in class in some extremely difficult questions that will never appear in both mid-term exam and final exam.
comment_grading:
I can only describe this in one word: DISASTER!

I HATE absolute grading policy in this course! 😡

Grading policy:
-Assignment: 10%
-Mid-term exam: 35%
-Final exam: 55%

Here are some statistics (I only have the average mark) :
-Assignment 1: 88.46/100
-Assignment 2: 94.25/100
-Assignment 3: 92.94/100
-Assignment 4: 88.93/100

-Assignment 6: 90.4/100
-Assignment 7: 82.19/100
-Assignment 8: 85.88/100

(Here is the website to check your result:
https://www.math.ust.hk/intranet/grading/student/
)

:(

For your reference, here are my 2021 HKDSE exam results in mathematics related subject:

Mathematics (Core): Level 5*
Mathematics (M2): Level 5

You can see that there are much more Mathematics elites than I have expected are studying in MATH1013. If your mathematics is bad, then I can only say, "Good luck.".
comment_workload:
There are only 8 assignments, 1 mid-term exam and 1 final exam.

Each assignment contains about 10 to 20 questions. Some of them are very easy and some of them are quite hard. Each assignment takes you 1 to 2 hours to complete.

The mid-term exam contains 10 MC questions only. (Difficulty level: Moderate)

The final exam contains 18 MC questions and 3 long questions. (Difficulty level: Moderate to Hard)

You need to do more revision if you want good grade. I get a bad grade because I do too little revision.

title: How you perceive the course mostly depends on your previous calc background
comment_content:
Please have a look at the course schedule file I attached below.

In general, the course mostly covers basic calculus topics: functions, limits and some related problems, derivatives and many related problems, integration (only u-sub, integration by parts is in MATH1014).

How hard this course is mainly depends on your previous background. If you have been through a math-heavy curriculum like M1/M2 (or like most Asian countries' curriculum), the concepts will be familiar to you and only revising for exercises is needed (tbh, the hardest questions on MATH1013 exams are still easier than some questions on my national high school graduation exam in math). Otherwise, chances are that you may struggle, but self-studying through Youtube or Khan Academy may be really helpful.
comment_teaching:
Teaching was a bit boring (maybe since I've already dealt with these contents in high school) but in general prof Wang seems to be among the better instructors.

Indeed, in the lectures, the prof goes through the contents and exercises at a steady pace so it is not hard to catch up with her. Her notes are really detailed and organized. She has a mainland accent but generally easy to follow. Prof. Wang is also really nice, so a bit sad that only about 1/3 students regularly attend her lectures.

Tutorials were not as good as the tutor in my section organizes his notes poorly (he writes and draws everything using his bad handwriting, so the notes look quite unattractive, especially the graphs). His accent is bad. The only good thing about tutorials is that there are more advanced exercises not covered during lectures, which you can utilize for self-studying (though a few problems in exams can be even more difficult than that)
comment_grading:
Assignments: 10%
Midterm: 35%
Final: 55%

Assignments (on Classviva): unlimited attempts are permitted, so basically free points for everyone. There might be hard questions, which you might want to consult with your friends, ask your tutor/prof or go to the Math Support Centre to get help for.

Midterm in previous years have a mix of MCs and long questions, but for my year there were only 10 MCs, so 1 mistake can cost you 3.5 points in the final grade. I also agree with previous reviews that there should be more questions in the midterm.

The final exam has a mix of MCs and long questions (for my year, 19 MCs and 3 long questions). Not too difficult. I finished everything, reviewed all the questions and submitted after just 2 hrs.

Previous reviews suggest that this course uses absolute grading.

My scores:
Assignments: 100 (mean: 92.85)
Midterm: 90 (mean: 57.79)
Final: 97 (mean: 66.44)
Total: 94.85 (mean: 66.05)
Grade: A+
comment_workload:
Pretty light if you have studied calc in high school, the only things requiring attention are to do the Classviva assignments before deadline and to put some effort into reviewing advanced exercises (especially the ones from past papers). I personally didn't even review at all in the days leading to the final exams :D

If you haven't had any idea of calc before, more effort might be needed.

author: jerry_nguyen
title: Bad Instructor
comment_content:
Please refer to other comments. Basically the same with MATH 1012, calculus, and stopped at Integral.

The content is quite a lot, in my opinion.
comment_teaching:
Miss Qin's teaching style is too bad, not to say is the worst. She sighed too much, and she just read from slides. Her explanation is hard to understand, and her English accent is hard to comprehend. She should study LANG 1002 to improve her English accent.

If you want to study, recommend Dr TK Lam's class. Dr Lam's teaching is the best in this course.

If you want to self-study, you just randomly pick a section.

TA's is good. Miss Xueyang always replies my email, and her English is near native. Should study in her class if you need assistance.
comment_grading: Grading is fine. However, A-range is difficult. >85 means A-range.
comment_workload: Workload is normal in this course. What you need to do is remember that there are homework in classviva and go and solve them. However, if you want to get good grades, you should solve more exercises because what appears in classviva is not enough for you to revise before final test.

author: abcdefghijklmno

As a M1 student, I would say it is quite hard to catch up in the very beginning. But if you do all the assignments by yourself, you will eventually be familiar with these topics.

In fact, I solve most of the problems on homework by watching youtube videos or searching in textbook instead of listening to him or his TA

author: happystar
title: Really bad score design - mid term = everything!
comment_content:
Syllabus:
1.  limit ( which dse m2 does not cover a lot)
2. differentiation
3. basic integration (excluding integrate by part, trigo substitution those m2 stuff)
4. some math theorem like mean value thm, intermediate value thm

Generally, the content is not that difficult, if you have taken m2, then everything should be all fine.  Pay more attention to limit part, really requires carefulness and m2 has little coverage on it.

( my mid-term die because of limit...)
comment_teaching:
The teaching of math profs or tutors is generally not very useful. But it is understandable.

Remember don't expect too much from prof, they are worked for research, not for teaching.

Highly suggested to learn by yourselves, which is much more efficient than attending lectures.~
comment_grading:
Rubbish grading.
Mid-term = everything, could it really distinguish student math ability or just test carefulness? Let us see this list:

from my 2 fd- mid term decide your score!
mid term:
90 ~ A
80 ~ A-
70~ B+ (me...)

BUT
mid term - 10MC only  (35%!!!!!!!!!)
final ~ 19 MC  3 LQ    (55% only...)

and note that the final is not that difficult, so cannot make a big difference with others. So losing more than 2 marks in mid-term is equivalent to no A. And for mid-term, it is all about carefulness.

My mark:
midterm: 70/100 (mean: 57.12)
final: 87/100   (mean: 69.27)
overall: 82.35 (mean: 67.34)
(83.5 have A-................)

B+ finally.......................

Finally, give you a cut off (not sure) for your reference:
>93.5 A+
88.5 A
83.5 A-
78.5 B+
72.5 B
67.5 B-
61.5 C+
54.5 C
47.5 C-
38 D
<38 F

The final tip to every freshman:

UST is big pressure, not because the things learned are difficult, but because the grading system is very harsh. Remember to take every mark that you can take because it really makes a difference if you want to get A,

不要輕視mid term, 你會後悔的！！！！！！
comment_workload:

avg 1-3h to finish 1 HW depend on different topics

author: non
comment_content:
limit, differential, integral

some trigonometric function
comment_teaching:
The instructor has a heavy accent.

And there are many mistakes in her notes, including typos and even confusing description of important concepts (I didn't notice that until I got a wrong answer in hw and check TA's notes)

TA (LIANG, Shixin) is very nice. It seems that she has been TA for many years. She will help you review some concept, and more importantly, supplement some TRICKs and exercise problems. So tutorial classes are of great help.
comment_grading:
Since it's a fundamental course, the overall grade should be good(A range is easy), but A+ is kind of difficult.

Midterm : 10 MC problem, total 100 scores, 10 each. 35% in total. Please prepare well for it, cuz only 1 or 2 mistakes may keep you away from A+.

Final : relatively better, about 20+ MC, 3 long question(some have partial credits). The points of each problem are smaller, so it allows you to make more(relatively) mistakes.

If you want to get A+, you have to get as high score as you can (i.e, 90/100, 95+/100, etc. I don't know the exact data).
comment_workload:
Theoretically, the workload is very small.

However, since problems in Midterm and Final exams will be much more difficult than hw and class, you are highly recommended to do some exercise for yourself. As mentioned before, you can go through those problems in TA's notes, which are actually from past paper(I suppose).

author: ThomasTse
comment_content: Syllabus: If you have taken HKDSE M1/M2, over 50% of curricula will be taught as you learned in your high school. Yet, you still have to revise, or else you still will fail your exam/midterm since many things to be taught are much more complicated than the HKDSE syllabus.
comment_teaching: The professor has the greatest skills to satisfy students' desire to sleep. If you have sleep deprivation, MATH 1013 will be a good course for you to have adequate rest to improve your physical health. Moreover, if you like math in HKDSE, you will not like math at university, since boring teaching style.
comment_grading:
There is fair grading since the university uses similar exam papers to be allocated to different students. It ensures the fair among students and it confirms academic integrity. Besides, one of the good points this course has is you can input your answer in Classviva, where you do your math homework, without limited opportunities. You can try unlimited times to ensure your homework is 100 marks before due. This makes that you can be easier to have a good grade in Math 1013.

However, things taught in tutorials and lectures and in notes are not enough for you to have a good grade in the course. If you want to have a good grade, I recommend you to find some group to find helps instead of revising alone.
comment_workload: If you are smart or have well prepared in HKDSE M2. The first few weeks(1-4) will be a good grade. However, when it comes to midterm exams, you will find that these you did and practiced in your homework and tutorial are not synonyms in mid-term exams or final exams. After those few weeks,  you will find that workload comes up.

author: WallKerSSS
comment_content:
Similar to m2. NO PROVE at all!
This is an introductory course in one-variable calculus. Topics include functions and graphs, limits of functions and continuity, derivatives and their applications, basic indefinite and definite integrals. Upon successful completion of this course, students should be able to: 1. develop basic computational skills in calculus; 2. express quantitative relationships using the language of functions; 3. apply the concepts and methods of calculus in modeling and problem solving.

Reading Materials:

Jishan Hu, Weiping Li and Yueping Wu. "Calculus for scientists and engineers with MATLAB". [Classviva]
James Stewart. "Single variable calculus: Early transcendentals". Cengage Learning, 2015. [Amazon]

author: susan72
comment_content:
Week

Topics

1

Number and intervals, inequalities and absolutes, functions and graphs, composite functions, exponential functions.

2

Trigonometric functions, inverse functions, logarithmic functions, inverse trigonometric functions.

3

Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws.

4

Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes, derivatives and rates of change.

5

Basic derivatives, product and quotient rules, derivatives of trigonometric functions.

6

Chain rule, implicit differentiation, derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and logarithmic functions.

7

Rates of change problems, related rates.

8

Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values.

9

Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs, L’Hôpital’s rule.

10

Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s Method.

11

Antiderivatives, areas and distances.

12

Definite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.

13

Applications of integrals, the substitution rule.
comment_teaching: Professor Ji is a very patient teacher. He gave detailed explanation to the content. He was always willing to answer our questions. So you can raise any question to him.
comment_workload:
Around 12 assignment.

You can do them repeatedly.
So no worries to get full marks.

Most of the students gained full marks.

author: solarski22
comment_content: Basic Calculus. A bit hard for DSE M1 students to follow.
comment_teaching:
Professor Li is good at teaching. I think he explained the knowledge quite well. Reg in his class will be a wise choice. Sometimes he will teach quite fast, especially if you are a M1 student. He is nice and you can always email him directly to ask him questions. He is patient and will explain clearly. Although sometimes he may not give you the full answer, it is still a good opportunity to learn. In zoom semester, he used his iPad to write his notes, and he will upload his handwritten notes later on. I think it is not enough by just reading his notes, you have to read the textbook at the same time. I think for M1 students, you can try to start reading the textbook (can be found online, or maybe you can try the attached notes below) or start to review the calculus part of your M1 syllabus. Prof Li is really a good professor, I remember he posted an additional zoom lecture for students who have not learnt  radian measures (in DSE M2 only).

The TA, Leung Ho Ming is also good. You can use Cantonese to ask him in his MSC hours, and he will explain it clearly. It is good to attend his tutorials before doing HW. His tutorials notes are useful too.
comment_grading:
This semester's exam is held online. No midterm. Only final and assignments counts towards your final grade. Scoring full marks in your homework will your grade a lot, and as there are unlimited attempts, it is therefore not hard to do so. The exam is a bit harder than the homework, you will have to really understand the content thoroughly to get good scores.

- Examination duration: final exam 3 hrs
- Percentage of coursework, examination, etc.:
   25% by homework
   75% by final exam
- The grading is based on students’ performance in assessment
   tasks.

For normal semesters, the grading will be different.
comment_workload: Understanding the concepts and practicing will be one of the major workload. Doing the HW is also one of them. The workload is appropriate for a 3-credit course.

comment_teaching: The English of the professor is not really good. You have to guess what he was talking about and that is kind of a waste of time. However, he is passionate about teaching, his sense of responsibility worths an aprise
comment_grading: Bad grading
comment_workload: webwork every week

author: Ming Mak
title: VERY POOR pronunciation and BORING as hell
comment_content:
Most of the content is in HKDSE M2 Syllabus, so it could be a waste a time for those who want to learn new things but took M2. Yet it is pleasant for those who want a good grade.

1 Number and intervals, inequalities and absolutes, functions and graphs, composite functions, exponential functions.
2 Trigonometric functions, inverse functions, logarithmic functions, inverse trigonometric functions.
3 Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws.
4 Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes, derivatives and rates of change.
5 Basic derivatives, product and quotient rules, derivatives of trigonometric functions.
6 Chain rule, implicit differentiation, derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and logarithmic functions.
7 Rates of change problems, related rates.
8 Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values.
9 Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs, L’Hôpital’s rule.
10 Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s Method.
11 Antiderivatives, areas and distances.
12 Definite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
13 Applications of integrals, the substitution rule.
comment_teaching:
His pronunciation have significant CHINESE ACCENT, with VERY POOR pronunciation that even with 120% concentration, you can hardly understand what is he speaking.
Also, he uses mostly the example in the textbook to teach which makes it boring as hell.
I struggled for a few weeks, and I decided to skip ALL of the lectures. It is COMPLETELY A WASTE OF TIME. Sleeping is much more worth than talking his lectures

TA's accent is also heavy but at least understandable, yet he mostly gives us a set of exercise for us to do in Tutorial and just go through the questions. While other TA go through the homework.
Not very useful so skipped all of them after few tutorials
comment_grading:
Absolute grading.
75% of the grade is from Exam while 25% is from Homework.
That means if you screw up your exam, you'll probably fail the course.
I got a B, with 77.41% totally.
comment_workload:
Light workload with a total of 8 homework, each with around 10-15 questions on Classviva.
And just a Final Exam with 2.5h, consist of 3 parts.

author: riried
title: Could be good grade
comment_content:
Differentiability, Continuity, Intermediate Value Theorem, Mean Value Theorem, 'one-to-one' functions, Sandwich(Squeeze) Theorem, Linear Approximation, Newton's Method, Extreme Points, Rolle's Theorem, L'Hopital Rule, Riemann Sum, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus

Typical university math content. For those who are taking it in the future: could read more extra materials for better insight down the line, perhaps the textbook will provide a better view of the theorems and what is going on in general since the professor will not be able to cover too much during the lectures.
comment_teaching: The professor I had is a little difficult to understand since the accent is not up there. Though I think he does have heart to teach.
comment_grading: The grading is fair in ordinary semesters, but during the latter half of the semester it switched to online mode, which i think has the grading scheme slightly more demanding. For the exams, they are quite straightforward as the majority are computational questions.
comment_workload:
Classviva: online homework which is quite easy

Exams may need more revision since the online semesters have started and to get an edge over others, you need to get a lot of questions correct.

author: Nicole001
title: 挺難的
comment_content:
主要偏向M2，冇讀M2嘅話(例如我)就要早少少惡補下M2，如果唔係會聽到一舊雞。

內容上主要都係derivatives同integral calculus，不過就用limit嘅方法去證明，會有d深同難明。
comment_teaching: 個professor挺有心教書，雖然口音有d怪，一開始我唔係好聽得明，不過聽耐咗就明。每次落堂之後都會留低解答問題，同埋將個堂嘅notes放上去canvas，可以隨時溫番個堂嘅野，係一個熱愛教學嘅阿sir。
comment_grading:
個人認為難攞靚grade，計分計功課同final(印象中, 各佔幾成我唔記得咗)，唔使take attendence。

grade俾分方法係根據你同班同學嘅mean，所以你同班同學平均分高，而你低分，就好容易攞C。
comment_workload:
還好，一星期交一次功課，每次大概十幾題，夠時間做，仲要可以不斷retake，計分計最高個次。

基本上你對derivatives同integral calculus熟嘅話，做題唔會太辛苦。

不過每次落堂之後最好抽時間溫書，如果唔係會容易跟唔切。

The final exam is separated into 3 parts

I guess you have to get 0-1 question wrong to get an A+

author: Serena17172
comment_content:
Generally speaking, the content is really the basic and
fundamental knowledge of university mathematics.There will be some textbook and lecture notes on Classiva for you to refer to.For me, it is very useful to consider these lecture notes as a tool for preview: after a quick scan, if I found I already know the
general logic and some examples of this chapter, then that will be perfect; if not, I will go through some concepts and detailed
-examples to have a overview of this chapter. Because of the style of the teaching part, it is indeed important.
comment_teaching:
For the teaching part, during the online teaching mode, all you can see is the screen-sharing of a draft paper(and the face of the professor), and the professor will demonstrate the process of
certain examples from lecture notes on Classiva. It could be really boring and useless if you just staring at the screen, most of the times you end up feeling sleepy. However, if you take some draft paper and calculate on your own with the professor or ahead of the professor, it would be much better.
comment_grading: There is no midterm, for the homework part, there is no limit in terms of submission, you can get full score if you want. Final term and practice before final term really matters.
comment_workload: Weekly quiz after class, the workload is generally 1 to 2 hours aweek if your do preview and review regularly. the workload will have a slight increase from beginning to final.

author: vx
comment_content:
It is an introductory course in one-variable calculus.

Week 1
Number and intervals, inequalities and absolutes, functions and graphs, composite
functions, exponential functions.
Week 2
Trigonometric functions, inverse functions, logarithmic functions, inverse
trigonometric functions.
Week 3
Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function, limit laws.
Week 4
Continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes, derivatives and rates of
change.
Week 5
Basic derivatives, product and quotient rules, derivatives of trigonometric functions.
Week 6
Chain rule, implicit differentiation, derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and
logarithmic functions.
Week 7
Rates of change problems, related rates.
Week 8
Linear approximations and differentials, maximum and minimum values.
Week 9
Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs, L’Hôpital’s rule.
Week 10
Curve sketching, optimization problems, Newton’s Method.
Week 11
Antiderivatives, areas and distances.
Week 12
Definite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Week 13
Applications of integrals, the substitution rule
comment_teaching:
The teaching is good. Prof. LI is really patient and willing to answer students' questions.

He even discussed some extra exercises in the class which really help me a lot in preparing for the final exam.

The TA is super nice, he explains the solutions step-by-step so it is easy to follow. The tutorial materials are useful.
comment_grading:
25% by homework
75% by final exam

There are unlimited attempts for homework. The marks for homework are based on the attempt with the highest score so basically people will get full marks for homework.

Due to covid-19, the final exam was conducted online with only MC questions.
comment_workload:
There are 8 assignments in total.
The workload is acceptable.

As I've never learnt much calculus before, it took me hours (sometimes a few days) to complete the homework. Do email the prof or TA when you face difficulties in doing the homework.

author: Asagami Fujino
comment_content:
Just basic calculus things, though it goes deeper than calculus in JEE, most of them are high school stuff.
Limits, derivatives application, some integral, etc.
The coursebook is actually not that good. I would recommend "Calculus" written by Gilbert Strang, "The Calculus Lifesaver" and the "Thomas Calculus".
comment_teaching:
Her accent is not unbearable, but the teaching is quite bad. She basically reading the slides.
If you really want to learn something, taking tutorials should be enough. The TA actually teaches quite well, and she explains things quite clearly.
Besides, Highlights of Calculus (by Gilbert Strang from MIT) is probably a better choice if you regret skipping classes. (And his course is awesome!!!) Calculus videos by 3Blue1Brown is also a good source. (You can get a more intuitive view into what calculus really is)
You can also buy yourself a "The Calculus Lifesaver" or "Thomas Calculus", reading these two books and doing the exercises should be enough. (Unfortunately, the first book doesn't have exercises attached, at least for the Chinese translation.)
comment_grading:
Homework: 25%
Final exam: 75%

Final is all mc questions and I got 2 wrong.
Homework is near full.
Got A.
I think making 1 or less mistake can get you an A+, assuming your hw is full (which you should probably be)
comment_workload:
Not much homework and they're quite easy. For most homework, you can finish them easily in around 4 hours (for those with fewer questions, it's less than 3 hours)
But maybe you can find more exercises for yourself.

author: pauuu
comment_content:
Basically m2 stuff.
Topics of the course include:
 number and intervals, inequalities and absolutes,
 functions and graphs, graph transformations, composite functions,
 exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithmic functions,
 Trigonometric functions, inverse trigonometric functions.
 Tangent and velocity, the limit of a function,
 limit laws, continuity, limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes,
 derivatives and rates of change,
 basic derivatives, product and quotient rules,
 chain rule, derivatives of inverse functions, implicit differentiation,
 rates of change and related rates,
 linear approximations and differentials, Newton’s Method,
 maximum and minimum values, Mean Value Theorem, derivatives and graphs,
 L’Hôpital’s rule,
 curve sketching, optimization problems.
 antiderivatives, areas and distances,
 definite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus,
 applications of definition integrals, the substitution rule.
comment_teaching: Boring. But can attend other lectures.
comment_grading:
15% by online homework
35% by midterm exam
50% by final exam
Mid term and final around mean. Hw full mark.got a b-
comment_workload: Fair.  8 hw in total. Each have serval weeks to do.

comment_grading:
Scoring isn't bad. There were 13 MCs on the test, and each question has an appropriate time allocated. However, it is true that if you don't know how to solve a problem as soon as you see it, you won't be able to solve it during the exam period.

I got B

comment_grading:
There is homework, midterm exam and final exam count towards the grade. Most people get full mark for homework.

author: DrainGang
title: Get a khan academy account
comment_content:
limits, differentiation and integration (only up to substitution)

Mostly M2 stuff, so if you are a M1 student (like me), prepare to suffer. Get a khan academy account and just watch the videos on there to learn, its a lot easier to understand the videos and also its way more efficient than wasting 2 hours in your lectures when you can be doing something else. (Even if its just sleeping in your bed.)
comment_teaching:
Lecturer: barely acceptable, heavy accent, poor English, notes are NOT very good, lessons are a bit boring with pacing too slow. Would give him a B- for effort as I can at least see him trying to spice things up with jokes and stuff (even if they are not funny...)

He also only taught the lesson content so good luck on how to approach questions in your homework or exams.

TA: Awful, decent English but seems to be in quite the hurry every single tutorial as the tutorials always end 10-20 MINUTES before they should. Would answer some questions when asked. If this guy is your TA, just skip the tutorials.
comment_grading:
Midterm (35%), webwork (15%), Finals (50%)

Webwork: while time-consuming, the 15% it gives to the grade is almost free as you have unlimited amounts of retries to finish them, and you SHOULD finish them as everyone else probably does, if you don't, you will put yourself in a major disadvantage. I got all 15%.

Midterm: from the beginning all the way to newton's method, included both MCs and long questions, quite difficult. I got around mean.

Finals: conducted online with only MCs, includes ALL materials taught, notes are allowed, also quite difficult. I got around mean also.

Grading: Absolute grading unless they decide to change it to a curve, either way the grading WILL be very poor, getting mean will net you a C or maybe a C+? (feel like that's very unlikely.)
comment_workload: Medium to high, the webwork takes a while (3-4 hours per webwork, 15 in total) and do some past papers or questions before exams, quite a lot of effort if you are not the most talented at mathematics. Again, good luck if you took M1 in secondary school.

comment_content:

author: Vickkkyy
comment_content:
Basic calculus content

Prerequisite: Level 3 or above in HKDSE Mathematics Extended Module M1/M2.

Exclusion: AL Pure Mathematics, AL Applied Mathematics; Math1003, Math1012, Math1014, Math1018, Math1020, Math1023, Math1024.

Brief Information/synopsis:

This is an introductory course in one-variable calculus. Topics include functions and graphs, limits of functions and continuity, derivatives and their applications, basic indefinite and definite integrals.



Intended Learning Outcomes:

1
Develop basic computational skills in calculus.
2
Express quantitative relationships using the language of functions.
3
Apply the concepts and methods of calculus in modeling and problem-solving.

author: diuhitnds
title: Fk you always teaches wrong things
comment_content:
CONTENT

Calculus. Most of them are part of the  DSE-M2 syllabus. As this is a foundation course, the content of it is not challenging at all, so don't be too afraid if you are M1 in high school.
Here's a list of new things learnt in this course:
Continuity, Differentiability, Intermediate Value Theorem, Mean Value Theorem, 'one-to-one' function, Solving problems with absolute functions, Sandwich(Squeeze) Theorem, Linear Approximation, Newton's Method, Critical Point, Rolle's Theorem, L'Hopital Rule, Reimann Sum, Fundamental Theorem of Calculus, integration and differentiation of inverse trigonometric functions.
comment_teaching:
TEACHING

Prof Chiang's English is clear, and he is willing to answer student's questions. However, his writes on the whiteboard in a less organised way, so it's not easy to follow. The teaching pace is slow, and he put too much time on explaining the fundamental knowledge that both M1 and M2 and even DSE Maths core should have covered, especially functions... He should have put more explanation on new topics such as L'Hopital Rule and more...

I didn't attend any tutorials.
comment_grading:
GRADING

Semi-absolute grading. Much fairer than previous semesters.
15% WebWork 35% Mid-term 50% Final
Total score
85-100%-->A range
70-85%-->B range
50-70%-->C range
40-50%-->D
0-40%-->Fail
Me:
WebWork Full mark
Mid-term 63/100 (Mean: 57)
Final 74/100 (Mean:  47)
Total 70/100  -->B
quite weird as the mean is exceptionally low for my year so i guess that's why my grades is slightly better than the absolute grading system.
comment_workload:
WORKLOAD

Homework: Basically 1 WebWork(Online homework) every two weeks, each consists at most 18 questions. WebWorks before the mid term should be quite easy and can be completed in an hour. But those after the mid term are quite challenging so please arrange enough tine for it, don't be a deadline fighter like me. You can attempt rhe questions infinite times ,therefore, try to get full marks in them.
No attendance required for any lecture and tutorials
Exam: Do your best in the relatively simple mid-term to secure a better grade. All contents will appear in final exam, so don't forget to practice on early topics for the final.

author: fl0ra
title: Be careful if you want a good grade
comment_content:
Basically the content is high school knowledge, especially the content before midterm, so you may feel bored at the beginning. However, the content becomes much more difficult after the midterm. Take care.
Before midterm (partially)
Exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithms, trigonometric functions, definition of limit, limit at infinity and horizontal asymptotes, derivatives, related rates, Newton's method
After midterm (partially)
L'Hopital's rule, curve sketching, anti-derivatives, definite integrals, indefinite integrals, the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
comment_teaching:
MOY Allen is a patient professor with fluent English and neat handwriting. His lecture notes are clear and easy to understand. The pace is relatively slow, so sometimes I almost fell asleep and sadly miss out some important points. He is willing to answer students questions and write down every step for students to make sure they fully understand.
To be honest, tutorial is more helpful in terms of getting a good grade.
comment_grading:
I believe this course is absolute grade, which means you probably need 95/100 to get an A+. Therefore, in order to get a good grade, you need to be extremely careful and pay special attention to the details.
midterm 35% final 50% homework 15%
Provisional grade scale:   F < 40% < D < 50% < C's < 70% < B's < 85% < A's ( I believe A+>95%)
mine
midterm:93/100
final:87/100
homework: 15%/15%
turn out to be an A
comment_workload: You don't have to memorize lots of things and the questions on Webwork are quite easy and short for most students. My suggestion is that if you want to get a good grade, you should do some extra exercise and finish the tutorial worksheets which are similar to the questions in the exams.

author: amyriad
title: Don't make any mistake or you will lose a subgrade
comment_content:
M2 DSE stuffs mainly in first half
Exponential functions, inverse functions, logarithms
Trigonometric functions, Inverse trigonometric functions
Tangent and velocity
Limit, Continuity
Limit at infinity, asymptotes
Derivatives, Differentiation (Implicit and Explicit)
Exponential growth ***IMPORTANT***
Linear approximations
Newton's method
Maxima, Minima
Mean value theorem
Shape of a graph, Curve sketching, Optimization
L'Hopital's rule
Anti-derivatives, Definite integrals, Reimann Sum
Substitution, Indefinite integrals
Areas and distances
comment_teaching: Tsz Kin may make some mistakes in teaching and pause until he finds them out so please remind him in case you know the problem in order not to end the lesson late. It is better to understand the concept of the course materials and don't just rely on the stated examples in class.
comment_grading:
Semi-absolute grading which is VERY FRAGILE with any of your careless mistakes in the paper.
Semi-absolute means the absolute grading for grade range but curve in sub-grades.
A+ boundaries is extremely high (probably the boundaries is somehow between 95 and 97.5, so no TOLERANCE)
Grading system: https://intranet.math.ust.hk/grading/student (Intranet so you have to use Pulse Secure / Virtual Barn (Now also requires Pulse Secure) outside the campus or Wi-Fi network or Computer Barn inside the Campus in order to access it)

Homework: 15% (Remember to get full for this part!!!)
Mid-term Exam: 35%
Final Exam: 50%
No calculator allowed.

**Score are rounded to 1 d.p.first, so 84.96, 85.03 is also 85**
A range = 85~100
B range = 70~85
C range = 50~70
D range = 40~50
F = 0~40


The English can be an killer as it may contains some data that are not useful or some information that you may overread. Tsz Kin and TA is not very tolerant to small mistakes in the steps even the method is completely correct and the answer is just flawed in one single step they would not give any marks starting from that line and deduct 10 marks from your exam and steal your expected grade off a subgrade. (i.e. if the constant is missing in the first step due to the overreading of the question, then you are unlucky. They may introduce the constant inflow rate and a variable leaking rate so if you forget to add the inflow rate, your whole answer will be sucked by a single constant offset and every steps will not be kindly treated by the markers). How is it supposed that the thing that killed you is the texts but not mathematics?

As the final exam contributes to 50% score, a 10% deduction in final equals 5% in overall score and probably a subgrade deduction.
comment_workload:
Webwork: useful for practice but not so useful for grades.
(https://webwork.math.ust.hk/webwork2/2018_FALL_MATH1013)

No reminder to deadline so it is better to finish the assignment as soon as possible.
INFINITE chances (theoretically), so you can guess the answer of Multiple-Choices and Multiple-Checkboxes questions.
Each homework lasts for a month and consists of about 13 questions. There are 9 homeworks, 120 questions in total (time may overlap with answer homework)
Some answers are very ugly in closed form so Ctrl+C and Ctrl+V are your friends if you insisted not to use approximated values (remember to copy also the outer bracket pair of the repeated part so you will not get wrong).

author: Hydrargyrum
title: Good grade and easy to learn
comment_content:
For mainland students, it is quite easy to understand the content. It only covers the former part of calculus and it only teaches basic concepts and simple computing.
It contains:
Numbers, Inequalities, and Absolute Values
Function
Limit and Continuity
The derivative of a Function & Formulas of Differentiation
More Exercises for Sandwich Theorem and Evaluation of Derivatives
Applications of Differentiation: Rate of Change
Linear Approximation, Newton’s Method, Extreme Values
Mean Value Theorem, Shape of Graph and L’h𝐨̂ptal’s Rule
Technique for Solving Inequality & Curve Sketching
Antiderivatives, Reimann Sum and Fundamental Theorem of Calculus
and the exam mainly tests your ability in derivative and antiderivatives.
comment_teaching: I think the professor prepare for the class with intention and the notes are full of knowledge. Tutorials will give you enough questions to practice so it is of no use to worry about the exercise.
comment_grading:
quite easy to get A range if you paid enough work on this course.
if you get >85%, you will have A range. If you have 95% you will get A+.
15% webwork
35% midterm
50% final
comment_workload: Not very heavy. The only thing you need to do is to finish your web work on time. and have exercises and past paper before midterm and final.

comment_content: The content is essentially the similar thing to M2 DSE, and the worse part is that even you know in M2, you can struggle in terms of understanding the question. I recommend plenty of self-studying since you have to learn maths by self-practice anyway.
comment_teaching: Let's be honest here, Professor Moy doesn't really teach here, he just says the concept and did it with very little elaboration. comment_grading:
Your grade is divided into the following pattern:
35% Mid Term
50% Final Exam
15% WebWork

I essentially perfected the WebWork, yet I performed quite substandard in both midterm and final, as I got the score below mean a little bit. In the end, I got C-.
I suggest you guys not to procrastinate when doing the WebWork, I know many of my classmates have a problem finishing their WebWork because they are simply too lazy! The homework can be easily completed once you grabbed the concept properly.
No need to worry about attendance, since I mentioned that it would not be included in your grade. I know a friend who almost constantly skipped the class, and he mostly does other things in class.
comment_workload:
Professor Moy doesn't give any extra assignments at all, all you need to do is to complete the WebWork.
If you want good grades in your exams though, again, self-study is the key. I suffer some not so satisfactory grades simply I didn't spend much time on study.

comment_teaching:
For the lecture session, he had a strong accent in speaking English, which made students difficult to understand what he said. Basically, he went through the PowerPoint slides from the textbook publisher. The PowerPoint slides were uploaded to Canvas after each lecture for reference.

For the tutorial session, colour-printed notes with explanations and past paper examples were given to students. She also gave us some time to go through the examples before explaining the answers. Students could also ask questions outside the classroom when the tutorial session ended.
comment_grading:
Grading scheme:
WeBWorK: 15%
Midterm exam: 35%
Final exam: 50%

No attendance required for lectures and tutorials.

Statistical data of Section T09B:

WeBWorK:
Homework 1: Mean 97.92/100
Homework 2: Mean 97.61/100
Homework 3: Mean 96.82/100
Homework 4: Mean 93.49/100
Homework 5: Mean 94.95/100
Homework 6: Mean 90.73/100
Homework 7: Mean 94.62/100
Homework 8: Mean 90.12/100
Homework 9: Mean 91.23/100

Midterm Exam: Mean 62.17/100

Final Exam: Mean 51.58/100

Overall: Mean 61.67%

Grade distribution:
93.5 < A+
88.5 < A < 93.5
83.5 < A- < 88.5
78.5 < B+ < 83.5
72.5 < B < 78.5
67.5 < B- < 72.5
61.5 < C+ < 67.5
54.5 < C < 61.5
47.5 < C- < 54.5
38 < D < 47.5
F < 38

Absolute grading.

All cut-offs seem to be lower compared to the original distribution.
comment_workload:
This course covered many topics taught in M2 and also several new topics which were difficult. Since I studied M1 in the past, there was a heavier workload for me comparing to other M2 students.

For the WeBWorK assignments, a new exercise was assigned about every week. Each exercise consisted of about 10 questions, some of which were challenging. However, since there were unlimited attempts and no time limits, it was not difficult to get full marks in these assignments. You could search online to find the solutions easily if you didn't understand the question. Just remember to mark down the homework deadlines on a calendar, since the due dates are quite irregular and it is easy to forget completing them before the deadline.

For the Midterm and Final exams, they consist of about 20 MC questions and several long questions. No calculators were allowed. It is recommended to finish the sample papers beforehand, which had a similar format comparing to the exam questions. Also, some WebWorK questions were similar to those in the exams, so understanding them also helps you to prepare for the exams.

inverse trigonometric functions (1.6)

trigonometric functions (3.3)

and logarithmic functions (3.5, 3.6)

minimum values (4.1)
9 Mean Value Theorem (4.2), derivatives and graphs (4.3), L’Hopital’s rule (4.4)
10 Curve sketching (4.5, 4.6), optimization problems (4.7)

12 Definite integrals (5.2), the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (5.3)

comment_content:
University Calculus course (Part I).
Quite many DSE M2 Content so it is an advantage for M2 students. For M1 students, good luck to you. :)
• Numbers, inequalities and absolute values (Appendix A)
• Coordinate Geometry and Lines
• Basic functions and graphs, composite functions (1.1, 1.2, 1.3)
• Exponential functions (1.5)
• Trigonometric functions (Appendix D)
• Inverse functions, logarithmic functions and inverse trigonometric functions (1.6, Appendix D)
• Tangent and velocity (2.1)
• The limit of a function, limit laws (2.2, 2.3)
• Continuity (2.5)
• Limits at infinity and horizontal asymptotes (2.6)
• Derivatives and rates of change (2.7)
• Basic derivatives (2.8, 3.1)
• Product and quotient rules (3.2)
• Derivatives of trigonometic functions (3.3)
• Chain rule (3.4)
• Implicit differentiation, derivatives of inverse trigonometric functions and logarithmic functions (3.5, 3.6)
• Rates of change problems (3.7, 3.8)
• Related Rates. (3.9)
• Linear approximations and differentials (3.10)
• Newton's method (4.8)
• Maximum and minimum values (4.1)
• Mean Value Theorem (4.2)
• Derivatives and the shape of a graph (4.3)
• L'Hopital's rule (4.4)
• Curve sketching (4.5, 4.6)
• Optimization problems (4.7)

comment_teaching:
Dr. Lam is already regarded as one of the best lecturers for this course. (Because I have never attended other Math 1013 sessions so I don't know how "bad" other lecturers teach.)

Overall speaking, his teaching is clear if you pay attention in lectures. But quite boring for me (maybe just because I am not that interested in Math) and his handwriting is not clear. He also mentions a lot of proof and things that will not be tested in the exams. Good if you love math so much though.

However, I do have to say his TA (Phyllis LIANG) is very good. She explains how to do the questions clearly. Quite exam-oriented as well as she will discuss past exam questions with us during her tutorials. She is very nice and willing to answer questions as well. Also, she always points out the common mistakes made by students.
comment_grading:
Absolute Grading.
85% from uniform exams = 35% Midterm + 50% Final Exam
15% from online WeBWork homework
Grade scale:   F < 40% < D < 50% < C's < 70% < B's < 85% < A's

In other words, if the paper is easy, you can get a good grade.
However, if in your semester the paper is difficult, you will get a poor grade. like me :(
So what grade you will get largely depends on your luck.

Of course, if you are extremely good at Math, you can get a good grade anyway so just ignore.
comment_workload:
9 set of Homework on WebWork with unlimited attempts.
Remember to finish all before the deadline so that you get full mark for your homework part and prepare for your exam.

author: Never Social
title: Basic math course, too basic tbh.
comment_content:
Refer to other posts for topics included.

Math1013, for students with M1/M2 background you say? More like treating you as junior highschool students! Hell, a too long amount of time is used to explain even the simplest concepts that as M1/M2 students should already know/ more then capable of understanding in an instant.

"Online homework" is done over at Webwork. Should be easy enough, keep inputing different answers until correct then bingo bongo get the marko. Just remember to do them, the weekly deadlines aren't regular so keep in mind.

Good thing is, the mid-term/ exam tends to assess abstract concepts rather then your computational prowess (no calc allowed in final) so if you know the whole syllabus and attended all the tutorials (which i didn't), all g.

All and all ez course.
comment_teaching:
Boi this lecturer just bore me to death. His careless mistakes, his/ this course's slow place. He uploads his handwritten paper used in lectures online, though good luck figuring out what he wrote and which topic is being covered if you didn't attend the lecture. I didn't bother attending his lectures since wk 5 or so.

Tutorial wise, the first wk gave me a bad impression (worksheets too easy, explainations too long) so I didn't attend the rest of them, which proved to be a mistake as some question types in the finals are new to me.
Not enough qualification to grade the TA.

Teach yourself.
comment_grading:
My friend once told me that if a concrete grading system that lists out score vs corr. sub-grades aren't given at the start of the semester, then it isn't really absolute.

Item (weighting, score):
Homework (0.15, 0.80) I'm a game addict who forgot to do his hw aight?
Mid-term (0.35, 0.9)
Final (0.5, 0.75)

Overall 81%, B+
Meh, fair is fair. Could have done better.
comment_workload: None if you have M2 background and wishes to get a B.

author: LizZy
comment_content:
the course started from SET to the FUNDAMENTAL THEOREM OF CALCULUS, containing Sandwich(Squeeze) Theorem, Linear Approximation, Newton's Method, Intermediate Value Theorem, Mean Value Theorem,  Rolle's Theorem, L'Hopital Rule, Reimann Sum, etc.


they are relatively easy. for many students may have already studied them before.
but it still may contain something you have not learnt in high school
comment_teaching:
Dr. Chang is willing to answer question. you can ask him during the break(we have a 3-minute break every lecture) or after class. and for mainland students, you will feel comfortable for you can ask your questions in Mandarin.

however,the examples he used is a little too easy(though they are already enough for you to understand the knowledge),so if you want to get extremely good grades, you should either be already very good at math or pay more attention to the tutorial questions.
comment_grading:
this course is absolute grading.
15% online homework(with unlimited attempts)
35% Mid-term
50% Final
-Total score
85-100%-->A range
70-85%-->B range
50-70%-->C range
40-50%-->D

attendance is not required

author: Tommy Li
title: Not good but not bad
comment_content:
This is an introductory course in one-variable calculus. Topics include functions and graphs, limits of functions and continuity, derivatives and their applications, basic indefinite and definite integrals.

Just a little bit more than M2 syllabus, namely mean value theorem, extreme value theorem, newton's method and l'hospital rule. The content is not difficult for normal M2 students. Practice more on the new topics and you will be fine. :)

Once I managed to prove "n-degree polynomial have at most n roots" using M.I. and the Rolle's theorem (mean value theorem) myself. I really get amazed about the usefulness of MVT and the approximation methods taught in this course.
comment_teaching:
I would say her teaching is boring (mainly because of her tone and the content) but it is very clear. She did point out explicitly some of the important points in midterm/exam during class. There are some practice time during the lesson  which is quite good for people that bad at math.

I especially like her hand written notes which the logic is clear and easy to follow. The examples are useful too.

I sometime felt asleep in class, but it is easy to pick up the progress. Even reading the notes suffice if you are the kind of person who hates to attend lesson. (I did attend every lesson tho)

The TA, Kenneth Ng, is really good too. He explains the concepts in details and I feel his passion in teaching. But sadly he got resigned after this semester.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading with no tolerance. Although the mean for the exam is quite low for this semester, the grading still adhere to the standard math grading scheme.

100% ≥ A+/A/A- ≥ 85% > B+/B/B- ≥ 70% > C+/C/C- ≥ 50% > D ≥ 40% > F

HW: 15/15
Midterm: 86/100 (Mean: 66.8)
Exam: 74/100 (Mean: 53.66)
Overall: 82.1/100 B+

Most non-honor version of UST courses are competition for carefulness. The problems are not that hard and it is not that difficult to get an A range. I lost 6 points in exam just because I blacken the wrong answer on the MC answer sheet. Another 9 points because of forgetting to flip the sign or chain rule during differentiation. It would have been an A- if I have been more careful.
comment_workload: Each week we have to complete 1 exercise of about 10 problems in webwork. Not much workload overall.

author: grateful grade
comment_content:
It's mostly dse m2 topics.
Week 1 Review of the concepts of functions and graphs.  (1.1-1.3; Appendix A.)
Week 2 Elementary functions.  (1.4-1.5; Appendix D.)
Week 3 Tangent and velocity; the limit of  a function and limit laws.  (2.1-2.3.)
Week 4 Continuity; limit at infinity; limit definition of derivative and rates of change.  (2.5-2.7.)
Week 5 Derivatives of basic functions; differentiation rules.  (2.8, 3.1-3.3.)
Week 6 Chain rule and implicit differentiation.  (2.8, 3.1-3.3.)
Week 7 Rates and related rates.  (3.7-3.9.)
Week 8 Linear approximation and differentials; Newton's method; Maximum and minimum values.  (3.10, 4.8, 4.1.)
Week 9 Mean Value Theorem; derivatives and the shape of a graph; L'Hopital's Rule.  (4.2-4.4.)
Week 10 Curve sketching; optimization problems.  (4.5-4.7.)
Week 11 Antiderivatives; areas and distance travelled.  (4.9, 5.1.)
Week 12 Definite integrals; Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.  (5.2-5.3.)
Week 13 Net change; substitution rule.  (5.4-5.5.)

People who studied m2 before will have great advantage in this course
comment_teaching: Tklam is willing to answer our questions after lectures. The teaching is bit boring tho
comment_grading: It's absolute grading. 15% webwork, 35% midterm , 50% final exam. can get good grades upon enough practices of past papers. Need to note that MC questions worth a lot in the examinations. Need to be very careful when doing multiple choice questions
comment_workload: very low, just need to do webwork once a week. It's very easy since we have unlimited attempts for each questions

author: WaiHei
comment_content: It's not very hard as long as you spend some time on it. If you have done all the webwork with full understanding, it will be fine and you won't get a bad grade in the midterm or final.
comment_teaching: Teaching is very poor that I don't recommend you to take the lesson. Instead of spending that time studying yourself will be a better option.
comment_grading: The grading is quite harsh that is fixed for a range of F< 40% < D < 50% < C < 70% < B < 85% < A instead of distributed by a curve. But if you have studied hard, you can get a good grade
comment_workload: The workload is fine. You may need to hand in one homework within two weeks and the difficulty of the homework is not very high.

3.9 Related Rates

comment_teaching: The notes of Prof Ye are really clear. She has a strong Chinese accent but then she speaks quite slowly, so it is easy to understand. However, since the notes are so clear, you can basically skip her lectures and go through the notes by yourself at home. I would say that you shouldn't skip the lectures if you didn't study M2 in DSE.
comment_grading:
It's absolute grading.

There're quite similar to the actual exam.
The grade will be great if you have studied M2. For M1 students, if you work hard I think it shouldn't be difficult for you :D
comment_workload: The webwork is easy. You have unlimited attempts for all questions (unlike Mastering Physics), just make sure that you get the correct answer at the end. Just ask your peers or go to the maths support centre whenever you encounter a problem.

author: phdoggo
comment_content: Those introductory calculus topics. It's cumbersome but necessary. Spring content are the same as Fall content, check this somewhere else.
comment_teaching:
I am totally impressed and motivated by prof. Chiang's teaching. He aware that the class is made up of underperformer in maths. Rather than challenging materials, he introduced math and calculus in a general manner. With detailed steps and proofs in his note, He shows calculus is nothing fancy mathemagic but a humble tool derived from simple axioms to solve problems.

He encourages students to reason calculus on a philosophical level, challenging our fundamental perception of the world. he waved a huge impact on me and make math once interesting again.

This is not any kind of advertisement but a heartfelt gratitude and appreciation to professor Chiang.
comment_grading: The grading in the spring semester is much less harsh than the summer, due to the general low performance. Mean is less than 50%, hence getting close to 60 %already yields an A.
comment_workload:
weekly webwork assignment which, if you recall from the fall semester 1013, are made up of the exact identical problems.

weekly tutorials are single page worksheets with demonstration by TA in class. It is though helpful.

author: chpoonag
comment_content:
It is basically about calculus. If you studied M2, it should be pretty easy.

Week 1 Review of the concepts of functions and graphs.
Week 2 Elementary functions.
Week 3 Tangent and velocity; the limit of a function and limit laws.
Week 4 Continuity; limit at infinity; limit definition of derivative and rates of change.
Week 5 Derivatives of basic functions; differentiation rules.
Week 6 Chain rule and implicit differentiation.
Week 7 Rates and related rates.
Week 8 Linear approximation and differentials; Newton’s method; Maximum and mini-
mum values.
Week 9 Mean Value Theorem; derivatives and the shape of a graph; L’Hopital’s Rule.
Week 10 Curve sketching; optimization problems.
Week 11 Antiderivatives; areas and distance travelled.
Week 12 Definite integrals; Fundamental Theorem of Calculus.
Week 13 Net change; substitution rule.
comment_teaching: It may be boring for the lecture as theories and proofs were explained in class. Only few time was about doing questions. However, I would say that Prof Hu is quite modest. You can point out anything he may have done wrongly during class directly. Additionally, he is quite patient in answering students' questions after class. He can spend half an hour answering our questions in another room after the lecture. Every time, there were about 5 people asking him, ranging from the local and the mainland students.
comment_grading:
Absolute grading.

15% homework (WeBWork)
35% midterm
50% final

There are both MC and long questions in the midterm and final exam papers, unlike those papers of this course in earlier years.

Be careful in MC questions in midterm. Each account for 5 marks.

There are unlimited attempts in WeBWork without any mark deduction.
comment_workload: Not high. The WeBWork is easy for those who have grasped the basic knowledge in secondary schools already. For M1 students, you may have to pay more effort as the topics are not entirely covered in college.

Midterm 35% (72/100, mean:68)

Webwork 15% (full marks)
comment_workload: Mainly from Webwork

author: occheung
comment_content:
This course covers the fundamental aspect of calculus.
Topics:
Functions, limit and derivatives, differentiation rules, differentiation applications, basic integrations.
some featured topics: Inverse functions, continuity and differentiability, linear approximation, mean value theorem, optimization.
comment_teaching:
This lecturer has very heavy mainland accent. So some pronunciation are quite off the standard.

However, he can improve your math concept quite well, especially on connecting different topics conceptually. His teaching pace is quite fast and will leave the last lecture before mid-term/final as review section (try out exam paper)
He also provides extra past paper through canvas, which is great for practicing and getting a grasp of the difficulty of the exam.
comment_grading:
Homework: 15%
Mid-term: 35%
Final: 50%

Mid-term and exams are both quite easy.
Getting all MC questions right makes your chance of getting a strong grade much higher. In most case, MC questions accounts for half of the score of a test or exam. It is very important to be careful on those.

Long questions can be very clumsy, but still easy.

My score:
Midterm: 97/100
Final: 94/100
Total score: 95.95/100
I got A+ for this course.
comment_workload:
Workload is not high if you have good mathematics foundations or even good knowledge of calculus.
Honestly, it is just high school calculus and slightly bit harder.
However, practices does help for quite a bit.
The only workload is homework, mid-term and exam.

author: Nicole MA
comment_content: The content is very easy during the add-drop period, and then it gets harder. Because there is no ppt before the class, you have to attend the lectures and take notes. There is no assignment and project, no attendance is required, and overall the content is not very difficult.
comment_teaching: TK is patient and always makes things quite clear for everyone to understand. However, that makes him sometimes too slow to draw attention. He never uses ppt, so he writes in class and uses the projector. His writing is not always clear, which may be hard to recognize if you do not attend the lectures or pay full attention to what he says.
comment_grading: The grading is not by curve, so get a good mark, and a good grade is granted. There are plenty of past papers and make sure you practice with them before the exams, because they help a lot, and sometimes the questions are similar.
comment_workload: Being a fundamental course, MATH1013 is easy enough to understand and perform well with medium attention and work. However, you do need to complete all the 10 homework online, which can be torturous as you type in answers over and over again, for a long period of time.
